proposals.title: Dynamic Adaptation of Cultivar and Management Parameters to Climate Change: A Multi-Model Protocol for Projecting Wheat Yields in the 21st Century
proposals.motivation: Current multi-model crop yield projections under climate change often assume static cultivar traits and management practices, contributing to high inter-model variability and potentially unrealistic estimates of future food security. This limitation is critical, as farmers continuously adapt by selecting new varieties and adjusting practices like planting dates and irrigation. A robust, standardized protocol for simulating dynamic adaptation is needed to reduce uncertainty and improve the realism of long-term projections.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a dynamic adaptation protocol, where cultivar parameters (e.g., phenology, heat tolerance) and key management decisions (e.g., sowing date, irrigation triggers) evolve in response to simulated future climate signals, will significantly reduce inter-model yield variability and produce more plausible projections than static assumptions.
proposals.method: We will develop and test a formal adaptation protocol within the AgMIP/GGCMI framework, focusing on wheat. The protocol defines rules for dynamically calibrating cultivar parameters in the DSSAT-CSM and APSIM models using climate indices (e.g., growing degree days, heat stress frequency) from downscaled CMIP6 projections for major wheat-producing regions. Management rules will adjust sowing dates based on temperature thresholds and initiate supplemental irrigation based on projected soil water deficits. The protocol's impact will be evaluated by comparing yield distributions, inter-model variance, and adaptation pathways from simulations with static vs. dynamic assumptions across multiple future scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the crop modeling community with a reproducible, transparent protocol for simulating autonomous on-farm adaptation, directly addressing a key source of uncertainty identified in multi-model studies. It will yield new insights into how the efficacy of different adaptation strategies (genetic vs. management) may vary across future mega-environments, informing breeding programs and climate resilience policy.
proposals.novelty: While past studies have tested discrete adaptation scenarios, this research integrates a systematic, climate-responsive recalibration of cultivar parameters—a core model assumption typically held constant—with adaptive management within a unified protocol, moving beyond one-off sensitivity analyses to a continuous adaptation framework.

proposals.title: A Statistical Early Warning System for Detecting Emergent Climate Stress Impacts on Wheat Yield Using Multi-Model Ensembles
proposals.motivation: Crop model intercomparisons show high variability in yield projections, complicating the timely identification of when and where climate stresses (e.g., waterlogging, heat) will transition from background noise to significant yield impacts. Pre-2026, epidemiological frameworks successfully detect disease outbreak emergence from noisy surveillance data, but these methods are not adapted for crop model ensembles. An early warning system for yield impact emergence is critical for proactive adaptation in vulnerable mega-environments.
proposals.hypothesis: Statistical algorithms used for detecting infectious disease outbreak signals can be adapted to identify the emergence of significant negative yield impacts from climate stressors within multi-model simulation ensembles, providing earlier and more robust signals than simple ensemble means.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Early Aberration Reporting System (EARS) and Cumulative Sum (CUSUM) control chart algorithms to analyze time-series output from a calibrated multi-model ensemble (e.g., AgMIP Wheat Protocol) run under historical and future climate scenarios. The algorithms will be trained to detect statistically significant deviations in simulated yield distributions across key production environments, using waterlogging stress as a primary test case. Model parameters sensitive to waterlogging will be calibrated against observed yield trial data from paddy field systems to ground-truth the warning signals.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel protocol for translating epidemiological early warning concepts to agronomic forecasting, directly addressing sources of variability in crop model studies. It will deliver a operational framework for identifying 'yield impact emergence hotspots' to prioritize field trials and adaptation research.
proposals.novelty: While early warning systems exist for pests and diseases, applying their core statistical frameworks to detect the emergence of yield impacts from abiotic stresses within multi-model ensembles is a novel, interdisciplinary synthesis. This moves beyond mean yield projection to diagnose the timing of significant impact onset.

proposals.title: A Calibration Protocol for an Integrated Crop-Livestock-Fishery Model to Assess Climate Resilience in Circular Agricultural Systems
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, agricultural system models largely operate in silos, focusing on crops, livestock, or aquaculture separately. This limits the ability to assess the true resilience and resource-use efficiency of integrated, circular farming systems, which are critical for sustainable intensification, especially in tropical environments. A scalable framework that connects these components is needed to evaluate synergies, nutrient cycling, and climate impacts holistically.
proposals.hypothesis: A robust, stepwise calibration protocol for a coupled crop-livestock-fishery model will reduce inter-model variability in system-level yield projections and reveal climate-resilient configurations that outperform single-enterprise systems in terms of total food production and nitrogen use efficiency.
proposals.method: We will extend the DSSAT crop modeling framework, leveraging Hoogenboom's expertise in crop model calibration and mega-environment protocols. The method involves: 1) Coupling calibrated DSSAT crop modules (e.g., wheat, cassava) with a livestock production model (e.g., based on nutrient requirements and manure output) and a pond aquaculture model (e.g., simulating fish growth from crop residues and manure). 2) Developing a sequential calibration protocol, first for individual components using existing data (e.g., crop trials, livestock performance), then for key integrative parameters (e.g., N transfer efficiency, waterlogging effects from pond seepage) using data from documented integrated farms. 3) Applying the calibrated model across a gradient of climate environments to identify optimal system configurations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, open-source modeling framework for integrated agri-aquaculture systems and a formal calibration protocol to enhance its reliability. It will provide quantitative evidence on how circular designs can buffer against climate variability and increase system-level productivity, directly informing the design of resource-efficient farms.
proposals.novelty: While crop-livestock systems have been modeled, the explicit integration of pond aquaculture into a major crop modeling platform (DSSAT) with a dedicated calibration protocol for nutrient and water flows is novel. This moves beyond simple coupling to a focused methodology for reducing uncertainty in multi-component system projections.

proposals.title: Inverting Adaptation Priorities: A Multi-Model Framework to Breed Wheat for Protein Stability Under Climate Stress
proposals.motivation: Climate change threatens global wheat production not only through yield loss but also by degrading grain protein content, a critical determinant of nutritional quality and economic value. Current breeding and crop model calibration overwhelmingly prioritize yield stability, risking the development of cultivars with poor nutritional resilience. This gap necessitates a paradigm shift in adaptation strategies to safeguard both food quantity and quality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by inverting the calibration priority to first stabilize grain protein content under variable climates, we can identify novel genotype parameter sets and mega-environments that maintain acceptable yield while ensuring nutritional security.
proposals.method: We will leverage the Agricultural Model Intercomparison and Improvement Project (AgMIP) protocols and a multi-model ensemble (e.g., DSSAT, APSIM) for wheat. A two-stage calibration will be implemented: first, models will be calibrated for key protein-determining parameters (e.g., nitrogen remobilization) using historical data across stress environments, including heat and waterlogging. Second, these parameterized models will be run under future climate scenarios to define new protein-stability mega-environments and evaluate associated yield outcomes, using sensitivity analysis to identify trade-offs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel calibration protocol and a set of model parameters explicitly targeting grain protein stability. It will produce maps of protein-based mega-environments to guide breeding trials and quantify the yield-protein trade-off landscape under climate change, offering concrete targets for breeding programs.
proposals.novelty: While multi-model studies have focused on reducing yield variability, explicitly inverting the calibration hierarchy to prioritize a quality trait like protein content under climate stress is a novel and non-obvious approach to crop model application and breeding strategy.

proposals.title: Integrating Crop Model Calibration with Network Analysis to Assess Global Wheat Trade Resilience to Protein Concentration Shocks
proposals.motivation: Global food security depends on stable wheat trade, but climate variability is increasing yield and quality uncertainty, particularly for grain protein concentration—a key nutritional and economic trait. Pre-2026 studies on multi-model variability and mega-environments highlight the need to link biophysical crop responses to systemic trade risks. A framework to trace protein shocks through trade networks is missing, limiting our ability to anticipate and mitigate nutritional disruptions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that coupling regionally calibrated wheat crop models with a global trade network model will reveal non-linear, systemic vulnerabilities in protein supply, where localized climate-induced protein deficits propagate disproportionately through specific trade pathways.
proposals.method: First, we will calibrate the DSSAT-CERES-Wheat model for major exporting regions using a multi-environment protocol, focusing on genotype-specific parameters governing protein response to waterlogging and heat stress. Second, we will simulate historical and future climate scenarios to generate probabilistic distributions of regional protein yield (yield * protein concentration). Third, these distributions will inform shock scenarios in a global wheat trade network model (using FAO Comtrade data and network resilience algorithms, e.g., cascading failure models) to trace protein deficit propagation and identify critical nodes and links.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, integrated modeling framework that quantifies climate-induced nutritional risk within the global food system. It will provide actionable insights for prioritizing breeding targets (e.g., stable-protein varieties) and trade policies to enhance nutritional resilience, moving beyond calorie-based trade analyses.
proposals.novelty: While crop models and trade networks are studied separately, this integrates them to trace a quality trait (protein), not just yield. It applies Hoogenboom's expertise in multi-model calibration and environmental stress protocols to a network resilience problem, a novel synthesis for pre-2026 science.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Crop Modeling and Machine Learning Framework to Predict Genotype-Specific Wheat Protein Content Under Variable Climate and Waterlogging Stress
proposals.motivation: Wheat grain protein content is a critical quality trait highly sensitive to genotype-environment (GxE) interactions, particularly under climatic stresses like waterlogging. Traditional crop models struggle to capture the genetic basis of these complex trait responses, limiting their utility for breeding. This project addresses the need for a predictive framework that integrates genomic data with process-based models to improve the selection of high-protein, climate-resilient wheat varieties.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating genomic markers with climate and soil data through a hybrid machine learning and crop modeling approach can significantly improve the prediction accuracy of genotype-specific wheat protein content, especially under variable waterlogging conditions, compared to using either method alone.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing multi-environment trial data for wheat, including genomic, phenotypic (yield, protein), and detailed environmental data. A calibrated DSSAT-CERES-Wheat model will simulate baseline crop growth and nitrogen dynamics. Machine learning models (e.g., Gradient Boosting Machines, Multi-Task Neural Networks) will then be trained to predict observed protein content, using simulated crop state variables from DSSAT, raw environmental drivers, and genomic markers as features. The framework will be validated across contrasting mega-environments, with a focus on paddy field systems prone to waterlogging.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, transferable protocol for linking genomic information to process-based crop model parameters, specifically for quality traits. It will deliver a predictive tool for breeders to identify genotypes with stable, high protein content under target production environments, including stress conditions. The research will also advance methods to reduce GxE-related variability in crop model ensembles by incorporating genetic covariates.
proposals.novelty: While ML has been used for genomic prediction and crop models for yield simulation, the hybrid integration of both to predict a complex quality trait like protein under specific abiotic stresses (waterlogging) is novel. This moves beyond calibrating traditional crop model parameters to directly learning the genotype-to-phenotype mapping for a trait poorly represented in process-based models.

proposals.title: Dynamic Sowing Date Optimization for Wheat Using Seasonal Climate Forecasts and Multi-Model Ensemble Simulation
proposals.motivation: Optimal sowing date recommendations are critical for maximizing wheat yield and resource use efficiency. Current recommendations rely on static historical climate probabilities, which fail to account for inter-annual climate variability and predictable seasonal signals, leading to sub-optimal decisions in individual years. Integrating dynamic, probabilistic seasonal forecasts can tailor sowing advice to the anticipated conditions of the upcoming season, addressing a key source of variability identified in crop model intercomparisons.
proposals.hypothesis: Sowing dates dynamically optimized using seasonal climate forecast ensembles will lead to higher simulated wheat yields and reduced risk of waterlogging stress compared to recommendations based on static historical climate probabilities. This benefit will be most pronounced in environments with high inter-annual climate variability.
proposals.method: We will employ the DSSAT-CSM-CERES-Wheat model, calibrated using Hoogenboom's established protocols for phenology and soil parameters. A multi-model ensemble of seasonal climate forecasts (e.g., from NMME) will be used to generate yearly, location-specific sowing date recommendations. These will be compared against static recommendations in a simulation experiment across diverse environments, with a focus on quantifying yield gains and reductions in waterlogging days. A key analysis will involve decomposing the variance in simulated yield outcomes attributable to the forecast-based sowing rule versus other model parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, operational framework for translating seasonal climate forecasts into actionable agronomic decisions, directly enhancing climate adaptation. It will contribute to the methodological debate on reducing variability in crop model studies by explicitly testing a dynamic management factor. The results will offer quantitative evidence for the value of climate services in temperate and tropical wheat systems.
proposals.novelty: While seasonal forecasts have been used for in-season management, their systematic integration for optimizing the foundational decision of sowing date remains underexplored. This proposal uniquely combines Hoogenboom's expertise in crop model calibration and mega-environment analysis with dynamic climate probabilities, moving beyond the static 'climate analogue' approach prevalent in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Assimilating Satellite Soil Moisture to Constrain Initial Conditions for Robust Sowing Date Optimization in Rainfed Wheat Systems
proposals.motivation: Optimizing sowing dates for rainfed crops is critical for climate adaptation, but recommendations are highly sensitive to the initial soil water profile at planting, which is often unknown. This uncertainty contributes significantly to the variability identified in multi-model intercomparisons, undermining farmer confidence. A method to provide a physically consistent, probabilistic estimate of initial soil moisture would directly address a key source of error in crop modeling protocols.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting an Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF) to assimilate remote sensing and in-situ soil moisture data into a soil water balance model will generate a more accurate and constrained probabilistic initial condition. This will, in turn, reduce the uncertainty in simulated sowing date outcomes when integrated with the CROPGRO-Wheat model and ClimProb seasonal climate analysis.
proposals.method: We will adapt a hydrological EnKF framework to interface with the soil water module of the DSSAT-CSM platform. The ensemble will be generated by perturbing key soil hydraulic parameters and forcing data. We will assimilate Level-2/3 soil moisture products from the SMAP and SMOS satellites, along with data from ground networks like the USCRN, prior to the simulated sowing window. The calibrated system will be tested across historical environments with documented waterlogging and drought stress to evaluate its impact on sowing date recommendations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, operationalizable protocol for initializing crop models that directly leverages the growing ecosystem of earth observations. It will contribute a methodological advance for reducing inter-model variability by improving state initialization. The outcome will be a more robust decision-support tool for tailoring sowing recommendations to specific field-scale conditions in rainfed agriculture.
proposals.novelty: While data assimilation is standard in hydrology and numerical weather prediction, its application for initializing operational *agronomic* decision models is novel. This research explicitly bridges this gap by adapting an ensemble data assimilation solution from flood forecasting to solve the critical, analogous problem of initial condition uncertainty in sowing date optimization.

proposals.title: Robust Sowing Portfolios for Wheat: A Finance-Inspired Optimization Framework to Manage Climate Risk Across Diverse Environments
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 multi-model studies, including those by Hoogenboom, reveal high variability in crop yield projections under climate uncertainty, complicating farmer decision-making. Current calibration and optimization often target average expected yields, leaving systems vulnerable to low-probability, high-impact climate extremes like droughts or waterlogging. A paradigm shift from maximizing mean yield to ensuring a robust minimum yield is needed for climate adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying robust portfolio optimization theory from finance to sowing schedules will identify diversified planting strategies (across dates and/or varieties) that provide a superior guaranteed yield floor under worst-case climate scenarios compared to strategies optimized for average conditions.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Conditional Value-at-Risk (CVaR) and minimax regret optimization frameworks. Using the DSSAT crop model, we will simulate wheat yield for a matrix of sowing dates and cultivars across a pre-2026 defined uncertainty set of historical and synthetic climate extremes (e.g., drought sequences, unseasonal heat). The optimization algorithm will treat each sowing date-cultivar combination as an 'asset' and solve for the allocation (e.g., percentage of field area) that maximizes the yield at the 10th percentile (CVaR) or minimizes maximum regret relative to a perfect foresight strategy.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, operational decision-support framework for robust crop calendar planning under climate risk. It will advance the methodological protocol for crop model application by integrating financial risk management theory, moving beyond sensitivity analysis to prescriptive, risk-averse optimization.
proposals.novelty: While crop model ensembles explore uncertainty, directly translating modern portfolio theory's robust optimization to physical sowing strategies is novel. This approach fundamentally re-frames the objective from expected yield to yield resilience, a critical shift for adaptation science.

proposals.title: Robust Agronomic Adaptation: A Multi-Model Framework for Optimizing Wheat Sowing Dates Under Deep Uncertainty in Extreme Temperature Response
proposals.motivation: Crop model intercomparisons, such as the Agricultural Model Intercomparison and Improvement Project (AgMIP), reveal high variability in yield projections under climate change, particularly for extreme temperature shocks. This variability stems from structural differences and uncertain parameters in non-linear yield response functions at high temperatures, undermining confidence in adaptation strategies like sowing date adjustments. A robust methodology is needed to design agronomic practices that perform well across both plausible climate futures and the range of model representations of crop failure thresholds.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating parametric uncertainty from extreme temperature response functions into a robust optimization framework will identify wheat sowing dates that are more consistently resilient across diverse crop models and climate scenarios than optimizations based on mean climate projections or single-model calibrations.
proposals.method: We will leverage the DSSAT-CSM framework, calibrating the wheat model (CERES-Wheat) for a target mega-environment using a standardized protocol. An ensemble of critical temperature parameters (e.g., cardinal temperatures, failure thresholds) will be derived from literature and multi-model studies to define a joint uncertainty set. This set, combined with a climate ensemble from CMIP5/6 for the region, will form the input for a robust min-max optimization algorithm (e.g., using linear programming or heuristic search) to find the sowing window that maximizes the guaranteed minimum yield across all plausible parameter-weather combinations. Performance will be tested against conventional optimization methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, actionable protocol for generating robust agronomic recommendations under deep uncertainty, directly addressing a key source of variability in climate impact assessments. It will advance the methodological integration of crop model calibration, uncertainty quantification, and decision-making under uncertainty for climate adaptation in agriculture.
proposals.novelty: The core innovation is the explicit treatment of parametric uncertainty in non-linear, extreme-temperature yield functions as a fundamental component of the robust optimization's uncertainty set, moving beyond uncertainty defined solely by climate projections. This directly targets the 'where models break down' problem highlighted in multi-model studies.

proposals.title: Calibrating a wheat crop model for field-level sowing date optimization using high-resolution soil and microclimate data
proposals.motivation: Current crop model applications for climate adaptation often use regional-scale data, leading to high variability in predictions and suboptimal recommendations for individual farms, as highlighted in multi-model studies. Precision agriculture requires site-specific management, yet robust protocols for downscaling process-based models to the field level using emerging high-resolution environmental data are lacking. This gap limits the ability to optimize key decisions like sowing dates to mitigate localized stresses such as waterlogging.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that calibrating a wheat crop model (e.g., DSSAT) with high-resolution, field-specific soil and microclimate data will significantly reduce prediction error for phenology and yield compared to regional calibrations, enabling reliable identification of optimal sowing windows for individual fields.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-environment trial (MET) framework across a network of commercial wheat fields with heterogeneous soils. For each field, we will collect high-resolution soil property maps (electrical conductivity, texture) and deploy microclimate sensors to capture temperature and soil moisture gradients. A protocol will be developed to calibrate cultivar parameters in the DSSAT-CERES-Wheat model for each field using a Bayesian calibration algorithm (e.g., Markov Chain Monte Carlo) informed by this localized data. The calibrated models will then simulate yield for a range of sowing dates under recent historical climate data to identify field-specific optimal windows.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel calibration protocol for field-level crop modeling, directly addressing sources of variability in model ensembles. It will deliver a practical framework for farmers to tailor sowing dates to within-field conditions, enhancing resilience to microclimate extremes. The research will extend the concept of mega-environments to the sub-hectare scale, bridging crop modeling and precision agriculture.
proposals.novelty: While Hoogenboom's team has defined mega-environments for cassava on paddy fields, applying a rigorous calibration protocol to downscale a process-based model for wheat to individual, non-uniform fields using real-time microclimate data is novel. The integration of Bayesian calibration with high-resolution sensor networks for sowing date optimization moves beyond regional climate response studies to true field-level decision support.

proposals.title: Sequential Data Assimilation of Real-Time Soil Sensor Data to Improve Dynamic Wheat Yield Forecasting Under Variable Environments
proposals.motivation: Crop model projections exhibit high uncertainty, partly due to errors in initial and evolving soil conditions, which are critical for simulating water and nutrient stress. While data assimilation (DA) is a cornerstone of modern numerical weather prediction for state updating, its application in operational crop modeling remains limited, especially for integrating high-frequency, real-time sensor data. This gap hinders the ability to provide accurate, in-season yield forecasts that can inform farm management and adaptation decisions under climate variability.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a sequential data assimilation framework (e.g., Ensemble Kalman Filter) to assimilate real-time soil moisture and temperature data will significantly reduce uncertainty in simulated soil states and root zone dynamics within a wheat crop model, leading to more accurate and reliable in-season yield forecasts compared to a model run without assimilation.
proposals.method: We will implement an Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF) to sequentially assimilate data from a network of in-field soil moisture and temperature sensors into the DSSAT-CERES-Wheat model. A pre-2026 calibration protocol will first establish baseline cultivar parameters. The EnKF will then update key model state variables (e.g., soil water content, soil temperature) at each assimilation step using the sensor observations, propagating uncertainty through an ensemble of model trajectories. The system will be tested across contrasting environments, including scenarios with waterlogging stress, to evaluate performance gains.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a functional, open-source framework for real-time crop model state correction, directly addressing the 'protocol' and 'parameter' uncertainty themes in multi-model studies. It will provide quantitative evidence on how much high-frequency soil data can constrain yield forecasts for wheat, contributing to more robust digital agriculture tools for climate adaptation.
proposals.novelty: While DA is established in meteorology, its operational use with real-time, proximal soil sensor data in crop models is nascent. This proposal uniquely integrates Hoogenboom's expertise in crop model calibration, environmental stress protocols, and mega-environment analysis with advanced sequential DA techniques to move from static calibration to dynamic, observation-driven simulation.

proposals.title: A Multi-Model Ensemble Framework for Regional Soil Organic Carbon Assessment in Wheat Systems Using Remote Sensing and Process-Based Crop Modeling
proposals.motivation: Quantifying soil organic carbon (SOC) sequestration at regional scales is critical for assessing agricultural climate mitigation potential, but current methods are limited by sparse ground measurements and high spatial variability. Process-based crop models can simulate SOC dynamics, but their application is constrained by the need for localized parameterization and high-resolution environmental data. This creates a significant gap between field-scale understanding and actionable regional-scale assessments for policy and farm management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating high-resolution remote sensing data (e.g., Sentinel-2 for LAI, soil moisture) with a rigorously calibrated ensemble of crop models (e.g., DSSAT, APSIM) will enable robust, spatially explicit estimation of SOC changes in wheat-based systems, reducing uncertainty compared to single-model approaches or purely empirical upscaling.
proposals.method: We will adapt the protocol from the AgMIP Wheat Team for multi-model calibration using historical yield and phenology data across diverse environments. Key soil and crop parameters influencing SOC turnover will be harmonized. The calibrated ensemble will be driven by spatially distributed inputs from remote sensing (vegetation indices, land surface temperature) and climate reanalysis data. A Bayesian model averaging approach will be used to weight ensemble members, generating probabilistic maps of SOC change for a target region (e.g., the U.S. Pacific Northwest). Validation will use existing soil survey networks and long-term experiment data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, scalable framework for regional SOC assessment that explicitly quantifies uncertainty through model ensembles. It will deliver a validated protocol for integrating remote sensing with process-based models to map carbon sequestration potential, directly informing land management and carbon credit schemes. The research will advance the methodology for creating 'mega-environment' maps for SOC, analogous to those used for crop yield trials.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a rigorous multi-model calibration protocol, developed for yield gap analysis, to the challenge of regional SOC upscaling, and fusing this ensemble with remote sensing data to constrain spatial simulations, moving beyond single-model studies or purely statistical extrapolation.

proposals.title: Improving Wheat Model Transferability: A Protocol for Calibrating Non-Linear Temperature Responses for Flowering Phenology
proposals.motivation: Current crop models often rely on linear thermal time accumulation (growing degree days) to predict flowering, a key determinant of yield. However, pre-2026 research highlights that this approach contributes to significant inter-model variability and poor transferability across diverse environments, especially under extreme temperatures and waterlogging stress. Accurately capturing non-linear, often supra-optimal, temperature responses is critical for reliable yield projections under climate change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that replacing the linear thermal time function with a calibrated non-linear temperature response function (e.g., a beta or skewed Gaussian function) will significantly improve the accuracy and reduce the bias of wheat flowering predictions across a global set of mega-environments, particularly under heat and waterlogging stress.
proposals.method: We will develop a standardized calibration protocol within the DSSAT-CSM framework, using the existing GLUE (Generalized Likelihood Uncertainty Estimation) or Bayesian calibration methods. The protocol will be applied to a global dataset of wheat phenology trials, spanning environments from the US to tropical paddy systems. The non-linear function's parameters will be estimated against observed flowering dates, and model performance will be rigorously compared against the standard linear approach using metrics of RMSE and AIC.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, empirically grounded method to reduce phenological uncertainty in wheat models, directly addressing known sources of variability in multi-model studies. It will yield a new set of calibrated parameters for major wheat cultivars, enhancing model utility for climate impact assessments and breeding for stress-prone mega-environments.
proposals.novelty: While non-linear temperature responses are known in physiology, their systematic integration and calibration into operational crop models for wheat phenology across commercial farm environments remains limited. This proposal uniquely combines a focus on model transferability with a practical calibration protocol for complex environmental stresses like waterlogging.

proposals.title: Field-scale flowering prediction for wheat using statistical downscaling of regional climate forecasts
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of key phenological stages like flowering is critical for managing crop inputs and adapting to climate variability. However, crop models like those in the Agricultural Model Intercomparison and Improvement Project (AgMIP) are often driven by coarse-resolution climate data, limiting their utility for on-farm decision support. Bridging the gap between regional climate forecasts and field-scale biophysical models remains a significant challenge for operational agriculture.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying a quantile mapping-based statistical downscaling technique to regional climate forecasts will generate field-scale weather inputs that significantly improve the accuracy and reduce the variability of wheat flowering date predictions from a calibrated crop simulation model.
proposals.method: We will use the DSSAT-CSM-CERES-Wheat model, calibrated with historical phenology and yield data from diverse environments. Regional climate forecasts (e.g., from NMME or CFSv2) will be statistically downscaled to field-scale using the quantile delta mapping (QDM) method, which corrects for distributional biases while preserving climate change signals. The downscaled temperature and precipitation will drive the calibrated model to predict flowering dates, with performance evaluated against observed phenology across multiple sites and seasons.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a robust, reproducible protocol for integrating seasonal climate forecasts into field-scale crop management tools. It will directly address sources of variability in multi-model studies by isolating the impact of input data resolution. The results will advance precision agriculture by providing a methodological framework for translating regional climate information into actionable agronomic guidance.
proposals.novelty: While downscaling is common in climatology, its systematic application to improve the spatial specificity of operational phenological forecasts using crop models is novel. This approach moves beyond using downscaling solely for climate change projections, applying it to the short-term forecast horizon critical for farm management.

proposals.title: A Machine Learning Framework for Predicting Wheat Phenology: Replacing Thermal Summation with Temperature Time-Series Analysis
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of flowering is critical for crop management and yield forecasting, yet traditional methods relying on summed heat units (e.g., growing degree days) often fail under variable environments, particularly with stresses like waterlogging. This contributes to the high model variability identified in multi-model studies. Machine learning (ML) from precision agriculture offers powerful tools to capture complex, non-linear relationships in environmental data that conventional crop models may miss.
proposals.hypothesis: Machine learning models trained directly on high-resolution temperature time-series data will predict wheat flowering dates with greater accuracy and robustness across diverse environments than models using aggregated thermal summation, especially under suboptimal conditions like waterlogging stress.
proposals.method: We will leverage historical wheat phenology and climate data from global trial networks, structuring daily temperature data as sequential inputs. Specific algorithms will include Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks to capture temporal dependencies and Random Forests as a robust benchmark. A key step will be a systematic calibration and evaluation protocol, comparing ML predictions against established crop model parameters (e.g., from DSSAT) under contrasting environments, including waterlogged conditions identified in prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, data-driven phenology module that can be integrated into existing crop modeling frameworks to reduce inter-model variability. It will deliver a calibrated, transferable protocol for applying time-series ML to phenology prediction, enhancing yield simulation accuracy for climate adaptation studies, particularly in stress-prone mega-environments.
proposals.novelty: While ML is used in precision ag, its direct application to replace fundamental phenological algorithms like thermal summation in process-based crop models is not standard. The novelty lies in fusing ML's pattern recognition strength with Hoogenboom's expertise in model calibration and environmental characterization to address a core source of model uncertainty.

proposals.title: A spatial-explicit calibration protocol for crop models to quantify yield impacts of within-field heterogeneity and edge effects in wheat systems
proposals.motivation: Crop models are foundational for yield forecasting and climate impact assessment, yet they overwhelmingly assume homogeneous, single-species stands, ignoring real-world spatial complexity. This limits their utility for precision agriculture, intercropping systems, and accurate yield estimation where field edges and soil variability create micro-environments. Addressing this gap is critical for improving model reliability and supporting sustainable intensification strategies like circular agriculture.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly representing within-field spatial heterogeneity and species interactions through a spatially nested modeling framework will significantly improve the simulation of crop yields and resource use (light, water, nitrogen) compared to standard homogeneous-area models.
proposals.method: We will extend the DSSAT-CSM framework by coupling it with a spatial grid module to simulate sub-field zones. The approach will: 1) Use high-resolution soil and drone-based canopy data to parameterize distinct zones within a field, including edges. 2) Implement a light competition algorithm based on the SAIL model to simulate intercropping (e.g., wheat-legume) and edge effects on radiation interception. 3) Apply a multi-environment calibration protocol, treating each spatial zone as a unique 'environment', to estimate key genotype-specific parameters for wheat under heterogeneous conditions, including waterlogging stress from adjacent paddy fields.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, open-source spatial calibration protocol for crop models, directly addressing known sources of inter-model variability. It will provide quantitative estimates of yield bias introduced by the homogeneous-field assumption and deliver validated methods to model intercropping and edge effects for improved farm-scale decision support.
proposals.novelty: While spatial crop modeling exists, a standardized calibration protocol for heterogeneous fields within mainstream models like DSSAT is lacking. The novelty lies in integrating a spatial competition module with a rigorous, multi-zone parameter estimation framework, moving beyond theoretical exercises to a practical tool grounded in Hoogenboom's work on mega-environments and calibration.

proposals.title: A Coupled Biotic-Abiotic Modeling Framework to Reduce Yield Prediction Uncertainty in Wheat Under Variable Climates
proposals.motivation: Crop model intercomparison studies, including those by Hoogenboom and colleagues, consistently identify biotic stresses as a major source of yield prediction uncertainty, often treated as static or independent factors. This limits the utility of models for climate adaptation planning, especially as changing precipitation patterns (e.g., increased waterlogging events) can dynamically alter pest and disease pressure. A mechanistic integration of biotic and abiotic stressors is needed to improve the reliability of yield projections for vulnerable crops like wheat.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dynamically coupling pest and disease modules with crop growth models, where biotic pressure is a function of crop phenology and microclimate (e.g., canopy humidity from waterlogging), will significantly improve the accuracy and reduce the variability of wheat yield simulations across diverse environments.
proposals.method: We will extend the DSSAT-CSM framework by integrating the WHEATPEST disease model and a generic insect damage module. The coupling will use a shared state-variable architecture: the crop model provides daily canopy microclimate and phenological stage (e.g., Zadoks scale), which drives infection and damage rates in the biotic modules. These modules then return daily damage coefficients (for leaf area, photosynthesis, and root function) that dynamically reduce crop growth parameters. The system will be calibrated and evaluated using historical wheat trial data from contrasting environments, including paddy fields prone to waterlogging, following a standardized protocol.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, open-source modeling framework that explicitly simulates climate-pest-crop interactions, moving beyond the common assumption of stress independence. It will directly address a key source of variability in multi-model studies and provide a tool for designing more resilient cropping systems under climate change, particularly for stress-prone mega-environments.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have added pest modules, our approach uniquely focuses on the feedback loop where crop status and abiotic stress (like waterlogging) create microclimatic conditions that govern biotic outbreak severity, thereby relaxing the critical assumption of independence between stress factors.

proposals.title: Assimilating Sentinel-2 LAI and Soil Moisture to Constrain DSSAT Wheat Simulations for Improved In-Season Yield Forecasting Under Variable Water Conditions
proposals.motivation: Multi-model studies highlight significant variability in crop model outputs, often due to uncertain initial conditions and parameterization, limiting their utility for in-season management and yield forecasting. Remote sensing provides spatially extensive observations of crop state (e.g., Leaf Area Index) and environmental conditions, but methods to dynamically integrate these data into process-based crop models like DSSAT remain underexplored in operational agronomy. This gap is critical for predicting yield under stress environments, such as waterlogging or drought, which are increasingly variable.
proposals.hypothesis: Sequential assimilation of remotely sensed Leaf Area Index (LAI) and surface soil moisture into the DSSAT-CERES-Wheat model using an Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF) will significantly reduce uncertainty in simulated crop state variables and improve the accuracy of end-of-season yield predictions, particularly in water-limited or waterlogged environments.
proposals.method: We will implement a formal data assimilation (DA) framework linking the DSSAT model with satellite-derived LAI (from Sentinel-2) and soil moisture (from SMAP or Sentinel-1). An Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF) will be used to sequentially update key model state variables (e.g., plant biomass, soil water content) at bi-weekly intervals during the growing season. The protocol will be tested across historical wheat trial datasets representing a gradient of water environments, including controlled waterlogging experiments. Sensitivity analysis will determine the most impactful observation types and assimilation timing for yield forecast skill.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a reproducible, open-source DA protocol for the crop modeling community, directly addressing sources of inter-model variability identified in recent studies. It will demonstrate a pathway to operationalize geoscience DA techniques for dynamic crop model calibration and in-season forecasting, enhancing the utility of models for precision agriculture and climate adaptation planning in variable environments.
proposals.novelty: While DA is established in geosciences, its application to constrain complex crop models like DSSAT for specific abiotic stresses (e.g., waterlogging) using a multi-variable (LAI + soil moisture) approach is novel. This moves beyond post-season calibration to active, in-season state correction, a significant advance for crop yield forecasting.

proposals.title: A Scalable Protocol for Regional Wheat Yield Forecasting by Aggregating Calibrated DSSAT Simulations Using Environmental Homogeneity Units
proposals.motivation: Global food security requires reliable large-area crop yield forecasts, but existing methods often lack the physiological detail of process-based models. Directly scaling site-specific models like DSSAT to regions remains a major challenge due to computational cost and spatial variability in soil, climate, and management. A robust, transparent scaling protocol is needed to translate the strengths of calibrated crop models for operational forecasting.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that regional wheat yield can be accurately forecast by aggregating DSSAT simulations run for representative sites within environmentally homogeneous units (EHUs), defined by key soil, climate, and landscape parameters, using a formal upscaling protocol.
proposals.method: We will develop a scaling protocol building on the 'mega-environment' concept. First, we will define EHUs for a target region (e.g., the US Midwest) using high-resolution spatial data on soil properties, historical climate, and topography. Within each EHU, representative management scenarios and cultivar parameters, pre-calibrated using historical trial data, will be selected. DSSAT will be executed for these representative points under seasonal weather forecasts. Finally, EHU-specific yields will be aggregated to the regional level using area-weighted averaging, with uncertainty quantified by propagating variability from within-EHU sampling, weather ensembles, and model parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, reproducible methodology for scaling physiological crop models, directly addressing the variability sources identified in multi-model studies. It will deliver a functional, spatially explicit yield forecasting system for wheat that integrates detailed cultivar calibration and stress responses (e.g., waterlogging) into a regional assessment framework.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of a formal environmental stratification protocol (inspired by cassava mega-environments) with pre-calibrated cultivar parameters and area-weighting rules to create a transparent bridge between site-level DSSAT fidelity and operational regional forecasting, moving beyond simple statistical aggregation or purely climate-driven models.

proposals.title: A Mechanistic Sub-Model for Within-Canopy Microclimate to Reduce Variability in Wheat Yield Projections Under Climate Extremes
proposals.motivation: A key source of variability in multi-model crop yield projections, as identified in pre-2026 studies, is the simplistic representation of canopy microclimate. Extreme climate events like heat waves and waterlogging have heterogeneous impacts within the canopy, affecting processes like pollen viability and disease pressure. Explicitly modeling the vertical gradients of light, temperature, and humidity is therefore critical for improving the physiological realism and accuracy of models under stress conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a mechanistic, multi-layer sub-model for within-canopy microclimate into existing crop models will significantly reduce inter-model variability and improve the simulation of wheat yield responses to compound stresses like heat and waterlogging.
proposals.method: We will develop and parameterize sub-models for light interception (using a Beer-Lambert law with sunfleck and diffuse radiation partitioning), air temperature (using energy balance and turbulent transfer theory), and humidity within a wheat canopy. These will be integrated into the DSSAT-CSM framework, leveraging its modular structure. The sub-model will be calibrated and validated using detailed micrometeorological and phenological data from controlled-environment and field studies, including waterlogging treatments, following established multi-model calibration protocols.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a reusable, physically-based module to enhance the process-level fidelity of major crop models. It will directly address a known source of projection uncertainty, leading to more reliable yield forecasts under climate variability. The resulting model will be a tool for designing climate-resilient management practices tailored to specific canopy micro-environments.
proposals.novelty: While 1D canopy models exist in ecology, their tight integration and calibration within the operational crop modeling frameworks used for agronomic forecasting, specifically to address model intercomparison variability for stresses like waterlogging, is novel. This moves beyond simple bulk canopy representations common in pre-2026 agricultural models.

proposals.title: Inverting the Crop Model: A Bayesian Optimization Framework to Derive Management Practices for Target Wheat Yields Under Climate Uncertainty
proposals.motivation: Crop models are traditionally used for forward simulation, predicting yield from management and environment. However, farmers and policymakers often work backwards from a yield target to determine necessary inputs, a complex inverse problem not well-supported by current tools. Addressing this gap is critical for designing climate-resilient, input-efficient cropping systems, especially in the context of variable environments and the need for precise management protocols highlighted in recent multi-model studies.
proposals.hypothesis: A systematic inverse modeling framework, combining a robust crop simulation model with Bayesian optimization, can reliably identify optimal or near-optimal combinations of planting date and input levels (e.g., nitrogen, irrigation) to achieve a pre-defined yield target across diverse climatic environments.
proposals.method: We will employ the DSSAT-CERES-Wheat model, calibrated using established protocols for key phenological and growth parameters. The inverse problem will be formulated as an optimization task: minimizing the absolute difference between simulated and target yield. A Bayesian optimization algorithm (e.g., using Gaussian Processes) will be implemented to efficiently explore the high-dimensional management space (planting date, N fertilizer rates, irrigation triggers) under historical and projected climate data for major wheat-growing regions. The framework will explicitly evaluate solutions under stress environments, including waterlogging scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, operational tool for generating management prescriptions tailored to yield goals and local climate. It will advance the field of agricultural decision support by formalizing the inverse problem approach, reducing the trial-and-error needed in management design. The analysis will also reveal how optimal management pathways to the same yield target diverge under different environmental conditions, informing adaptive strategies.
proposals.novelty: While crop model calibration (a parameter inverse problem) is standard, the explicit inversion for management variables to hit a yield target is not. This approach moves beyond scenario comparison to a targeted search, integrating advanced optimization techniques from machine learning with process-based crop modeling in a way not commonly practiced in pre-2026 agronomy.

proposals.title: Mapping Climate Analogs for Wheat Breeding: A Crop Model-Based Protocol to Identify Future Target Environments for High-Performing Genotypes
proposals.motivation: Crop breeding programs develop genotypes optimized for current mega-environments, but climate change is shifting these zones, risking the future relevance of elite genetic material. Pre-2026 studies highlight significant variability in crop model projections and the need for robust environmental characterization. This creates an urgent need to systematically identify future climates that match the conditions where today's best-performing genotypes excel, thereby informing breeding pipelines and seed deployment strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: By calibrating a multi-model ensemble for specific high-yielding wheat genotypes and applying a standardized environmental characterization protocol, we can identify robust geographic climate analogs for the mid-21st century, where these genotypes' optimal growth conditions are projected to occur.
proposals.method: We will calibrate the DSSAT-CERES-Wheat and APSIM-Wheat models using detailed phenology and yield data for three elite wheat genotypes from diverse current mega-environments. A standardized protocol, extending concepts from cassava paddy field studies, will define key environmental covariates (temperature, solar radiation, waterlogging stress, drought patterns). Using CMIP5 climate projections for 2040-2060, we will employ a multivariate climate-analog mapping algorithm to identify future geographic regions where climate covariates fall within the calibrated optimal ranges for each genotype.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide breeders and agronomists with a spatially explicit, model-informed map of future target environments for specific genetic material. It will advance methods for reducing uncertainty in climate impact assessments by linking genotype-specific model calibration to analog-finding techniques. The output is a practical decision-support framework for prioritizing breeding lines and planning trial networks under climate change.
proposals.novelty: While climate-analog studies exist, this approach is novel in being genotype-centric and driven by detailed crop model calibration of elite material, flipping the traditional focus from 'what genotype fits a future location' to 'what future location fits this genotype'. It integrates Hoogenboom's recent work on model variability, environmental protocols, and waterlogging stress into a targeted breeding application.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating Heat Sterility Risks for Wheat: A High-Resolution Climate and Crop Multi-Model Ensemble Analysis
proposals.motivation: Projections of future wheat yield under climate change often show significant variability, with some studies suggesting heat-induced sterility may be a secondary stressor compared to chronic heat and drought. However, these projections have largely relied on coarse-scale climate models that may inadequately represent the intensity and duration of sub-daily heat waves during critical flowering periods. This gap limits our ability to accurately assess a key physiological threat to global wheat production.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using high-resolution, bias-corrected climate projections that better capture peak temperature extremes will lead to crop model simulations showing significantly greater yield losses from heat sterility in key wheat-growing regions than previously estimated.
proposals.method: We will implement a multi-model ensemble using established crop models (e.g., DSSAT, APSIM) for wheat, focusing on parameterization of heat sterility response functions. These will be driven by a new generation of high-resolution (e.g., ~25 km) CMIP6 climate model outputs, statistically downscaled to represent sub-daily temperature spikes. A standardized calibration protocol, informed by historical yield trial data across diverse environments, will be applied to reduce inter-model spread. The analysis will compare yield projections from high-resolution and traditional coarse-resolution climate forcings.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more robust, spatially explicit quantification of future heat sterility risk for wheat, directly addressing known sources of variability in crop-climate projections. It will deliver a calibrated multi-model protocol for assessing extreme heat impacts, aiding in the identification of vulnerable mega-environments and informing breeding and adaptation priorities.
proposals.novelty: While multi-model studies exist, this work uniquely integrates the latest high-resolution climate projections explicitly designed to capture heat waves with a targeted, physiology-based model inter-comparison on sterility, moving beyond mean temperature approaches.

proposals.title: Calibrating Wheat Crop Models to Projected Rainfall Extremes to Reduce Yield Prediction Uncertainty in Rainfed Systems
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 climate projections indicate significant shifts in rainfall intensity and drought frequency, challenging the foundational assumption of stable water availability in rainfed crop modeling. This leads to high inter-model variability and unreliable yield projections, as highlighted in multi-model studies. Addressing this is critical for adapting agricultural systems and ensuring food security under climate change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly calibrating wheat model soil and plant water parameters against projected future rainfall regimes, rather than historical weather, will significantly reduce prediction uncertainty and improve the accuracy of simulated rainfed yields under climate change.
proposals.method: We will employ the DSSAT-CERES-Wheat model, leveraging Hoogenboom's expertise in calibration protocols. A multi-environment trial dataset, including waterlogging stress observations, will be used. Key soil hydraulic and crop drought tolerance parameters will be calibrated using a two-step approach: first against historical data, then perturbatively against future climate scenarios (CMIP5/6) emphasizing drought and intensity shifts. The Generalized Likelihood Uncertainty Estimation (GLUE) algorithm will quantify parameter and yield uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new calibration protocol for rainfed crop models under non-stationary hydroclimates, directly addressing a source of multi-model variability. It will deliver more robust mega-environment delineations for wheat under future climates, aiding breeding and adaptation planning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in shifting the calibration target from historical climate to projected future rainfall statistics, moving beyond the standard assumption of stable water availability. This integrates climate projection uncertainty directly into the crop model parameterization process.

proposals.title: Economic and Hydrological Feasibility of High-Temperature-Sum Wheat Varieties Under Climate Uncertainty
proposals.motivation: Projections of climate change impacts on crop yields often focus on biophysical potential, neglecting critical adoption constraints faced by farmers. Pre-2026 studies, including multi-model comparisons led by Hoogenboom, highlight vast variability in yield projections, partly due to omitted socio-economic and resource access factors. Integrating seed cost economics and irrigation water availability constraints is essential for realistic assessments of adaptive technologies like heat-tolerant varieties.
proposals.hypothesis: The adoption feasibility of high-temperature-sum wheat varieties is more strongly constrained by regional irrigation water access and seed cost economics than by their biophysical yield potential alone under future climate scenarios.
proposals.method: We will extend the DSSAT-CERES-Wheat model, calibrated using established protocols for parameters related to phenology and waterlogging stress. Model ensembles will simulate yields for standard and high-temperature-sum varieties across diverse environments, using CMIP6 climate projections. A spatially explicit adoption model will then integrate simulated yields with gridded data on irrigation water availability and a cost-benefit analysis factoring in seed premiums and water costs, using a constrained optimization algorithm to map feasible adoption regions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel framework that couples biophysical crop modeling with economic and resource constraints, directly addressing sources of variability in multi-model studies. It will deliver spatially explicit maps of adoption feasibility for heat-tolerant wheat, providing actionable guidance for breeders and policymakers on where new varieties are most likely to be viable.
proposals.novelty: While crop models are routinely used for climate impact studies, explicitly modeling adoption feasibility by dynamically integrating hydrological constraints and seed cost economics into yield projection ensembles represents a significant advance beyond standard biophysical analysis.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Non-Linear Interaction of Elevated CO2 and Extreme Temperature Stress on Wheat Yield: A Multi-Model Calibration and Mega-Environment Analysis
proposals.motivation: Projections of future crop yields under climate change often incorporate a CO2 fertilization benefit, but this is based on mean temperature responses. The increasing frequency of extreme heat events, which can directly damage photosynthetic machinery, may negate these benefits, creating critical uncertainty in food security forecasts. This interaction is poorly quantified in current crop models, contributing to the high variability in multi-model intercomparison studies.
proposals.hypothesis: The yield benefit of elevated atmospheric CO2 for wheat is significantly reduced or negated when extreme temperature stress coincides with critical reproductive growth stages, and this non-linear interaction can be systematically characterized through targeted parameter calibration.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-model calibration protocol using the DSSAT-CERES-Wheat and APSIM-Wheat models. Controlled-environment and field data (including from waterlogging studies to assess interacting stresses) on wheat growth under combined elevated CO2 (e.g., 550 ppm) and supra-optimal temperature regimes will be used to calibrate temperature response parameters in photosynthesis and phenology modules. A sensitivity analysis will identify the most influential parameters governing the CO2-by-extreme-heat interaction. These calibrated models will then be used to define new 'mega-environments' based on the joint probability of extreme heat and elevated CO2 conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, calibrated protocol for simulating the critical CO2-by-extreme-temperature interaction, directly addressing a key source of variability in crop model ensembles. It will deliver new functional relationships and defined mega-environments to improve the spatial targeting of climate adaptation strategies for wheat production.
proposals.novelty: While CO2 and temperature effects are often studied separately, this proposal integrates Dr. Hoogenboom's expertise in multi-model calibration and mega-environment analysis to explicitly target and parameterize their non-linear interaction, moving beyond simple additive assumptions prevalent in pre-2026 impact studies.

proposals.title: Quantifying Tail Risks in Wheat Yield Stability: An Extreme Value Theory Approach to Calibrate Crop Models for Catastrophic Failure
proposals.motivation: Current multi-model crop yield projections, as highlighted in pre-2026 studies, show high variability, particularly under extreme climatic stressors like waterlogging and heatwaves. This variability obscures robust risk assessment for low-probability, high-impact crop failures, which are critical for food security planning and adaptation strategy development. A formal statistical framework is needed to model the tails of yield loss distributions beyond the capabilities of standard calibration protocols.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying Extreme Value Theory (EVT) to the outputs of a multi-model ensemble for wheat will allow for the robust estimation of the probability of catastrophic yield loss under current and future climate scenarios, revealing non-linear thresholds missed by conventional calibration. We hypothesize that EVT-fitted parameters will identify specific environmental and management conditions that disproportionately increase tail risk.
proposals.method: We will utilize a large ensemble of simulated wheat yield data from the DSSAT-CSM framework, calibrated using established protocols for diverse environments, with explicit stressors like waterlogging. Instead of analyzing central tendencies, we will fit Generalized Extreme Value (GEV) and Generalized Pareto (GPD) distributions to the lower tail (e.g., lowest 10% of yields) from spatially and temporally aggregated model outputs. The Peak-Over-Threshold method will be applied to time series of simulated yield anomalies to model exceedances of critical loss thresholds. Model parameters (shape, scale, location) will be analyzed as functions of climate covariates (e.g., growing degree days, precipitation extremes) and soil parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, statistically rigorous method to quantify the risk of crop failure in crop modeling studies, directly addressing the variability issue in multi-model ensembles. It will produce spatially explicit risk maps for wheat production under climate change, informing the definition of 'mega-environments' based on failure risk rather than average yield. The calibrated EVT parameters will offer a new set of interpretable metrics for resilience and vulnerability.
proposals.novelty: While EVT is established in climatology and finance, its application to crop model ensembles for direct risk quantification of yield failure is novel. This moves beyond sensitivity analysis and mean-yield calibration to explicitly model the probability of system collapse, integrating statistical extremes with process-based agronomic modeling in a way not commonly done pre-2026.

proposals.title: Calibrating a Wheat Crop Model at Plot-Scale Using Farmer Management Data to Reduce Yield Prediction Uncertainty
proposals.motivation: Crop model intercomparisons, such as the Agricultural Model Intercomparison and Improvement Project (AgMIP), have consistently shown high variability in yield projections, partly due to inadequate representation of sub-field heterogeneity and actual farm management. This limits the utility of models for precision agriculture and localized climate adaptation planning. Incorporating detailed, site-specific management and soil data is a critical step to improve model skill for on-farm decision support.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that calibrating a wheat crop simulation model (e.g., DSSAT-CERES-Wheat) using high-resolution, farmer-reported management data and plot-specific soil properties will significantly reduce prediction error for yield and biomass compared to regional-scale calibration protocols.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing on-farm trial networks in the U.S. Pacific Northwest, collecting detailed management logs (planting date, cultivar, irrigation, fertilizer applications) and soil samples for key plots. The DSSAT model will be calibrated using a sequential protocol: first, genetic coefficients will be estimated using the Generalized Likelihood Uncertainty Estimation (GLUE) algorithm against plot-level phenology and yield data. Subsequently, soil water and nitrogen parameters will be optimized using the same algorithm, constrained by the local management data. Model performance will be validated against an independent set of plot data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a robust, transferable protocol for plot-scale crop model calibration using readily available farm data, directly addressing sources of variability identified in multi-model studies. It will demonstrate the value of farmer-centric data collection for improving the spatial precision of yield simulations, bridging the gap between research models and commercial farm operations.
proposals.novelty: While crop models are often calibrated for specific cultivars or regions, this approach systematically integrates the full suite of real-world, farmer-implemented management decisions and within-field soil variation into the calibration process, moving beyond research-station conditions to true farm environments.

proposals.title: Inverse Modeling for Climate-Resilient Wheat: Identifying Optimal Phenology and Heat Tolerance Traits for Future Mega-Environments
proposals.motivation: Crop model intercomparisons consistently show high variability in yield projections under climate change, partly due to uncertainty in cultivar-specific parameters. This complicates breeding and adaptation planning. Inverting the modeling process to define optimal trait combinations, rather than just simulating known varieties, provides a direct pathway to inform breeding programs for future climates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that inverting a multi-model ensemble for wheat will reveal distinct, non-intuitive combinations of phenological and heat tolerance parameters that maximize yield stability across future climate scenarios, and that these optimal trait sets define new target mega-environments for breeding.
proposals.method: We will employ the Agricultural Model Intercomparison and Improvement Project (AgMIP) protocols, using a calibrated ensemble of wheat models (e.g., DSSAT, APSIM). The inverse problem will be framed as an optimization: for defined future climate scenarios (including waterlogging stress), an evolutionary algorithm (e.g., NSGA-II) will search the parameter space for phenology and heat tolerance traits that maximize yield and stability. The analysis will be conducted across major global wheat-growing regions to map resulting optimal trait combinations to new mega-environments.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, target-oriented framework for climate adaptation breeding, moving beyond vulnerability assessment to solution design. It will deliver specific parameter sets for ideotypes and map the geographic regions (future mega-environments) where each ideotype would be most successful, directly addressing the variability challenge in crop modeling studies.
proposals.novelty: While crop models are routinely used for scenario analysis, their systematic inversion via multi-objective optimization to define breeding targets for future climates is a significant methodological shift. This approach integrates trait optimization with the mega-environment concept, creating a direct link between climate impact modeling and practical breeding program design.

proposals.title: Dynamic Cultivar Coefficients: A Framework for Calibrating Crop Models to Management Intensity and Technological Trends in Wheat Production
proposals.motivation: Crop model intercomparisons consistently show high variability, partly due to the assumption of static cultivar coefficients. In pre-2026 practice, coefficients calibrated for a cultivar under one management regime are often applied universally, ignoring how optimal phenology and growth parameters may shift with changes in input intensity, irrigation, or planting technology. This limits model accuracy for simulating yield trends and climate impacts across diverse farming systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that key cultivar coefficients for wheat (e.g., photoperiod sensitivity, grain filling duration) are not static but vary systematically with management intensity and technology adoption, and that a dynamic calibration framework will significantly improve yield simulation accuracy across contrasting environments.
proposals.method: We will use the DSSAT-CERES-Wheat model and a global dataset of wheat trials spanning low-input to high-intensity management. A multi-environment calibration protocol will be employed, grouping trials by a Management Intensity Index (MII) derived from inputs and technology use. For each MII group, cultivar coefficients will be calibrated. We will then develop and test regression models or a simple machine learning algorithm (e.g., Random Forest) to predict coefficient values as a function of the MII and key environmental covariates (climate, soil). The performance of this dynamic approach will be validated against the static coefficient method.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, empirically grounded framework for making cultivar parameters context-dependent, directly addressing a source of variability in multi-model studies. It will enhance the predictive capability of crop models for assessing yield gaps, technology adoption impacts, and climate change effects under evolving agricultural systems.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has focused on calibrating for environments or specific stresses like waterlogging, explicitly linking cultivar coefficients to a continuous spectrum of management intensity and technology trends represents a significant conceptual and practical advance in crop model parameterization.

proposals.title: Inverting the Thermal Response: Quantifying the Vulnerability of Cooler Wheat Regions to Extreme Heat Events
proposals.motivation: Crop model projections consistently indicate that warmer regions suffer greater yield losses from climate change, but this may overlook acute vulnerabilities in cooler, high-latitude regions. These cooler regions, often targeted for agricultural expansion or intensification, may host wheat varieties and systems less adapted to extreme heat events, which are increasing in frequency globally. This research addresses a critical gap in climate risk assessment by testing whether the established thermal response gradient holds under extreme event scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extreme heat events cause greater proportional yield losses in currently cooler wheat-growing regions compared to warmer regions, due to lower inherent heat tolerance of common cultivars and management practices. This inverts the canonical finding of greater climate change impacts in already warm areas.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-model ensemble (e.g., DSSAT, APSIM, SALUS) calibrated for major wheat cultivars using established protocols from the Agricultural Model Intercomparison and Improvement Project (AgMIP). The experimental design will simulate historical (1980-2010) and future extreme heat scenarios across a global transect of mega-environments, from cool (e.g., Northern Europe, Canada) to warm (e.g., India, Australia). Key phenological and stress response parameters will be perturbed in a sensitivity analysis to identify mechanisms. Yield impact will be analyzed as proportional loss relative to region-specific optimal yields.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a spatially explicit vulnerability map of wheat systems to heat extremes, challenging simplistic latitudinal gradients of climate risk. It will directly inform breeding programs and adaptation planning by identifying which cooler regions require most urgent development of heat-tolerant varieties. The study will also contribute to understanding sources of variability in multi-model climate impact assessments.
proposals.novelty: This proposal explicitly tests the inversion of a well-established macro-scale finding in crop climatology by focusing on extreme events rather than mean temperature shifts. It integrates the concepts of cultivar-specific parameter calibration, mega-environment classification, and extreme event analysis in a novel framework to reveal non-linear vulnerabilities.

proposals.title: Calibrating for Catastrophe: A Controlled-Failure Approach to Validate Wheat Yield Predictions Under Extreme Heat
proposals.motivation: Projections of crop yield under future climate change show high variability, especially for temperature extremes beyond historical ranges, due to divergent methodological assumptions. This uncertainty undermines adaptation planning. Drawing an analogy from materials science, where failure models are built from controlled stress tests, we propose a novel empirical framework to ground truth model extrapolations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that intentionally inducing yield failure in controlled environments will reveal non-linear physiological thresholds and breakdown mechanisms that, when used for calibration, will significantly reduce the divergence between statistical and process-based crop model predictions for extreme warming scenarios.
proposals.method: We will design a multi-environment controlled experiment for a major wheat cultivar, applying progressive heat stress regimes in growth chambers and field-based heat tents to push crops to complete yield failure. Response data will be used to fit and compare a Bayesian non-linear yield-temperature function and the DSSAT-CERES-Wheat model. A key step is calibrating DSSAT's cardinal temperature and stress response parameters directly against the observed failure points, followed by a validation protocol using independent data from recent heatwave-prone field trials.
