proposals.contribution: This work will produce a first-of-its-kind empirical yield-response curve validated into the failure domain, providing a benchmark for model evaluation. It will deliver a calibrated model parameter set for extreme heat and a diagnostic framework to explain why different modeling approaches diverge, directly addressing the variability question in multi-model studies.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in crop modeling for its deliberate induction of complete yield failure as a calibration target, moving beyond extrapolation of sub-optimal responses. It formally adapts the 'controlled failure testing' paradigm from materials science to agricultural climatology.

proposals.title: Coupling Agent-Based Adaptation with Crop Model Ensembles to Reduce Yield Projection Uncertainty
proposals.motivation: Current multi-model ensemble projections for crop yields under climate change show high variability, partly because they treat farmer adaptation as static or pre-defined scenarios. This ignores the dynamic, feedback-driven nature of real-world decision-making, where farmers observe and respond to changing conditions and risks. Integrating this human feedback loop is critical for improving the realism and utility of impact assessments for stakeholders.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating an agent-based decision model that dynamically triggers adaptation strategies (e.g., cultivar switching, irrigation investment) in response to simulated yield losses will reduce the spread of ensemble yield projections and produce more plausible impact trajectories for major crops like wheat.
proposals.method: We will extend established crop model calibration protocols (e.g., for DSSAT) to define key yield and stress thresholds. An agent-based modeling (ABM) layer, using a rule-based architecture, will be coupled to a multi-model ensemble (e.g., AgMIP-style). The ABM agents (representing farm decision-makers) will implement adaptation actions when ensemble-projected yields cross pre-calibrated thresholds for specific environments. These actions (e.g., altering planting dates or tolerance parameters) will feed back into subsequent ensemble runs, creating an iterative loop. The system will be tested across diverse climate and soil environments, including waterlogging-prone areas.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel modeling framework that explicitly quantifies how adaptive feedback alters climate impact distributions. It will provide a methodological advance for the AgMIP community by moving adaptation from a scenario input to an endogenous model variable. The results will offer more actionable risk assessments by identifying which impact thresholds are most likely to trigger significant agricultural adaptation.
proposals.novelty: While agent-based models are used in land-use studies, their tight, iterative coupling with biophysical crop model ensembles to simulate threshold-based adaptation feedback is novel. This approach directly addresses the call in Hoogenboom's recent work to better understand variability in multi-model studies by incorporating a dynamic human dimension.

proposals.title: Bayesian Constraint of CO2 Fertilization Effects Using Observational and Experimental Priors for Robust Wheat Yield Projections
proposals.motivation: Projections of future crop yields under climate change exhibit large uncertainty, a significant portion of which stems from divergent representations of the CO2 fertilization effect (eCFE) across models and empirical methods. This structural uncertainty undermines confidence in adaptation planning and food security assessments. A formal, data-driven approach to constrain this key process parameter is urgently needed to produce more reliable projections.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating observational data from historical yield trends and direct physiological responses from Free-Air CO2 Enrichment (FACE) experiments as informative priors within a Bayesian framework will significantly constrain the posterior distribution of eCFE parameters, leading to a more robust and probabilistically weighted multi-method ensemble of future wheat yield projections.
proposals.method: We will first conduct a structured sensitivity analysis to decompose yield projection uncertainty attributable to eCFE parameterization across selected crop models (e.g., DSSAT, APSIM) and empirical response functions. A Bayesian calibration protocol will then be implemented, using likelihoods from historical yield data (e.g., from the Global Yield Gap Atlas) and priors derived from meta-analyses of wheat FACE experiment results. Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling will be used to generate posterior distributions for eCFE parameters for each modeling approach. Finally, these constrained parameters will be used to run future climate scenarios, producing a probabilistically weighted ensemble projection.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantifiable reduction in the uncertainty of wheat yield projections attributable to CO2 response. It will deliver a transparent, reproducible protocol for constraining a critical but uncertain process in crop modeling. The resulting probabilistically weighted projections will offer a more robust evidence base for climate impact assessments and adaptation strategy development.
proposals.novelty: While multi-model ensembles are common, this research uniquely applies a formal Bayesian data assimilation framework to directly constrain the representation of a specific physiological process (eCFE) using distinct observational and experimental data streams, moving beyond simple model averaging to process-informed probabilistic weighting.

proposals.title: Quantifying Methodological Uncertainty in Wheat Yield Projections Under High-Warming Scenarios (2.0°C to 4.0°C)
proposals.motivation: Multi-model ensemble studies, such as the Agricultural Model Intercomparison and Improvement Project (AgMIP), have been crucial for quantifying climate impact uncertainty on crops, but their analyses have largely focused on warming levels near 1°C. As global climate policy and risk assessments increasingly consider higher warming scenarios (e.g., RCP8.5, SSP5-8.5), the uncertainty arising from methodological choices—model structure, parameterization, and calibration protocols—remains poorly quantified for these more extreme conditions. This gap limits our ability to provide robust risk assessments for food security under the most challenging future climates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the methodological uncertainty component of total projection uncertainty for wheat yields increases non-linearly with warming level, becoming the dominant source of uncertainty beyond 2.0°C of global temperature increase, due to model extrapolation beyond calibrated environmental ranges and divergent representations of extreme heat and compound stress.
proposals.method: We will leverage the existing AgMIP/GGCMI multi-model ensemble framework, applying a structured protocol to simulate winter and spring wheat yields across major global production regions. The analysis will use bias-adjusted CMIP6 climate data for scenarios corresponding to 1.0°C, 2.0°C, 3.0°C, and 4.0°C global warming levels. Methodological uncertainty will be decomposed using Analysis of Variance (ANOVA), isolating variance from model choice, cultivar parameter calibration (using established protocols from Hoogenboom's work), and the representation of key processes like waterlogging and heat stress. Sensitivity analysis will identify which process parameterizations contribute most to divergence at high temperatures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic quantification of how methodological uncertainty scales with warming intensity for a major global crop. It will produce actionable insights for model improvement by pinpointing processes whose representation causes the largest projection divergence under extreme warming. The results will directly inform climate adaptation policy and breeding programs by clarifying the confidence limits of yield projections for high-emission pathways.
proposals.novelty: While ensemble studies have analyzed uncertainty at moderate warming, this proposal explicitly focuses on the methodological uncertainty component under high-warming scenarios (2-4°C), a critical but under-explored range where models are pushed beyond their standard calibration environments, making the scaling of uncertainty non-obvious.

proposals.title: Disentangling Direct and Indirect Temperature Effects on Wheat Yield Using Structural Equation Modeling and Multi-Model Ensembles
proposals.motivation: Understanding the complex pathways through which temperature affects crop yield is critical for climate adaptation, yet traditional crop models often conflate direct thermal stress with indirect effects mediated by factors like waterlogging or phenology. This ambiguity contributes to the high variability in multi-model projections, as identified in recent syntheses. A method to formally partition these effects would improve model calibration and the design of targeted management strategies for specific environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant portion of the simulated yield response to elevated temperature in wheat is indirectly mediated by changes in soil water dynamics and accelerated phenological development, rather than by direct heat stress on photosynthesis and grain filling.
proposals.method: We will apply Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) to output from a calibrated multi-model ensemble (e.g., DSSAT, APSIM, CropSyst) for wheat across diverse global environments. The SEM will specify a priori causal pathways linking temperature to yield via intermediate variables (e.g., soil moisture, evapotranspiration, anthesis date). Model parameters will be estimated using maximum likelihood or Bayesian methods, and model fit will be evaluated with pre-2026 standard indices (CFI, RMSEA). The analysis will leverage existing multi-environment trial datasets and controlled simulation experiments from prior studies on waterlogging and mega-environment protocols.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework to attribute yield variance to specific physiological and environmental pathways, reducing epistemic uncertainty in model intercomparisons. It will yield a more mechanistic protocol for model calibration by identifying which processes require precise parameterization under different climate regimes, directly supporting the development of robust mega-environments for breeding and management.
proposals.novelty: While SEM is established in ecology, its application to partition causality within the *output* of process-based crop model ensembles, rather than solely observational data, is a novel synthesis. This approach moves beyond correlation to test explicit causal structures in silico, addressing a core limitation in interpreting multi-model studies.

proposals.title: Bayesian Model Averaging to Reduce Uncertainty in Multi-Model Climate Impact Projections for Wheat Yield
proposals.motivation: Multi-model ensembles in crop modeling, as highlighted in pre-2026 studies like the AgMIP Wheat project, show high variability in yield projections under climate change, complicating adaptation decisions. This variability stems from structural differences and parameter uncertainties across models. A formal, probabilistic framework is needed to synthesize these divergent projections into robust, weighted estimates for stakeholders.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying Bayesian Model Averaging (BMA) to an ensemble of process-based and statistical crop models will produce more reliable and better-calibrated probabilistic yield projections for wheat under diverse climate scenarios than the simple ensemble mean.
proposals.method: We will assemble a multi-model ensemble from existing protocols (e.g., AgMIP) for wheat, including models like DSSAT, APSIM, and SARRA-H, calibrated using Hoogenboom's established methods for parameters related to phenology, waterlogging, and stress. For each model, a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithm will be used to estimate posterior parameter distributions and model likelihoods given historical yield and environment data from global trial sites. BMA weights will be computed from these likelihoods and used to combine the posterior predictive distributions of future yield under CMIP6 climate scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a statistically rigorous method to quantify and reduce projection uncertainty in crop-climate studies, moving beyond ensemble means. It will deliver a transparent protocol for weighting models based on performance, directly addressing the question of 'Why is there so much variability?' and offering improved probabilistic risk assessments for agricultural planning.
proposals.novelty: While BMA is established in climate science, its application to weight entire crop model classes (process-based vs. statistical) within a unified probabilistic framework for yield projection is novel. It integrates model calibration, climate scenarios, and formal uncertainty quantification in a way not yet standard in agricultural modeling.

proposals.title: Downscaling Climate Vulnerability: A Multi-Method Ensemble Framework for Probabilistic, Farm-Level Wheat Yield Projections
proposals.motivation: Current regional-scale crop model ensembles, as analyzed in studies like the Agricultural Model Intercomparison and Improvement Project (AgMIP), reveal high variability in climate impact projections, limiting actionable insights for farmers and policymakers. This variability stems from both climate uncertainty and the failure to account for critical sub-grid heterogeneities in soil, water, and topography that govern farm-level resilience, particularly under extreme events like waterlogging. A method to probabilistically 'downscale' impact assessments to the farm scale is urgently needed to identify true vulnerability hotspots and inform targeted adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that nesting a calibrated wheat model ensemble within a dynamically and statistically downscaled regional climate model ensemble, and fusing outputs with hyper-local geospatial data, will significantly reduce projection uncertainty and generate robust, probabilistic maps of farm-level climate vulnerability that are more actionable than regional averages.
proposals.method: We will implement a two-tier downscaling and fusion protocol. First, we will apply the Weather Research and Forecasting (WRF) model for dynamical downscaling and a quantile mapping technique for statistical downscaling of CMIP6 climate projections for a target region. Second, we will nest the DSSAT-CERES-Wheat model, calibrated using established protocols for parameters related to waterlogging and phenology, within these climate ensembles. Model outputs will be fused with fine-resolution soil, digital elevation, and land-use data using a Bayesian model averaging approach to weight ensemble members, producing probabilistic yield loss and vulnerability maps at sub-field resolution.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, generalizable framework for high-resolution agricultural climate risk assessment that explicitly quantifies uncertainty from both climate and local biophysical factors. It will directly address the sources of variability in multi-model studies by providing a methodological pathway from regional projections to farm-level decisions, contributing to the development of 'mega-environments' for stress tolerance at a previously unattainable resolution.
proposals.novelty: While downscaling is standard in climatology, its systematic integration with a calibrated crop model ensemble and geospatial data fusion to create probabilistic, farm-scale vulnerability maps represents a novel translation of micro-meteorological methods to agricultural impact assessment, moving beyond sensitivity analysis to actionable risk quantification.

proposals.title: Calibrating Wheat Crop Models for Heatwave and Interannual Variability Impacts to Reduce Yield Projection Uncertainty
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 climate impact studies often rely on seasonal or annual mean temperatures, yet crop yields are disproportionately affected by short-duration extreme heat events and interannual climate variability. This mismatch contributes to the high variability in multi-model ensemble projections identified in prior research. A systematic calibration protocol targeting these transient extremes, rather than mean conditions, is needed to improve the reliability of yield forecasts under climate change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that calibrating key phenological and stress-response parameters in wheat crop models (e.g., DSSAT-CERES-Wheat) specifically against observed yield responses to heatwaves and interannual variability will significantly reduce model ensemble uncertainty for yield projections under future extreme event regimes.
proposals.method: We will leverage global wheat trial data spanning diverse environments, including waterlogging-prone paddy systems, to isolate yield impacts of discrete heatwaves. Using a multi-model ensemble (e.g., AgMIP Wheat Team models), we will implement a two-tiered calibration protocol: first, standard calibration for phenology under optimal conditions; second, a focused calibration of heat stress and drought parameters using years with identified extreme events. Statistical techniques like the Generalized Likelihood Uncertainty Estimation (GLUE) will be used to derive posterior parameter distributions conditioned on extreme event performance, directly addressing sources of inter-model variability.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, publicly available parameter set for wheat models, calibrated for extreme events, and a replicable calibration protocol for other crops. It will directly quantify the reduction in projection uncertainty achievable by shifting calibration focus, providing more robust yield mega-environments for breeding and climate adaptation planning under increased climatic extremes.
proposals.novelty: While crop model calibration is common, a dedicated protocol targeting parameter estimation during heatwaves and high-variability years, informed by the analysis of multi-model variability sources, is a novel approach to constrain the most uncertain aspect of yield projections.

proposals.title: Modeling Heterogeneous Poverty Impacts of Agricultural Modernization: A Multi-Model, Multi-Environment Scenario Analysis for Wheat Systems
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 literature often assumes market-oriented agricultural transformation uniformly reduces poverty, yet empirical evidence suggests complex, heterogeneous outcomes. This research gap is critical for designing equitable climate adaptation and food security policies, especially in vulnerable environments where crop models are used for foresight. A systematic, model-based exploration of adverse distributional effects is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that under specific biophysical and socio-economic environments, market-oriented agricultural intensification scenarios will increase simulated poverty incidence for sub-populations of smallholder farmers, primarily due to increased exposure to input cost volatility, yield risk from extreme weather, and waterlogging stress.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-model simulation framework (e.g., DSSAT, APSIM) for wheat, calibrated using established protocols for diverse environments, including those prone to waterlogging. Scenarios will integrate climate projections, input/output price volatility, and credit constraints. A key innovation is the explicit parameterization of 'poverty modules' within the modeling workflow, linking simulated net farm income distributions (accounting for production costs and risk) to standard poverty metrics for defined farm typologies.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a formalized methodology for integrating distributional economic outcomes into biophysical crop modeling studies. It will generate a typology of high-risk environments and farmer profiles where modernization could exacerbate poverty, providing a more nuanced evidence base for policy and development targeting.
proposals.novelty: While crop models are routinely used for yield and climate impact assessment, their explicit coupling with poverty outcome modules to test the *heterogeneous* welfare effects of agricultural transformation scenarios represents a novel, interdisciplinary synthesis of pre-2026 biophysical and economic modeling approaches.

proposals.title: Validating and Refining Climate Impact Projections for Wheat Using a Multi-Model Panel Calibrated with Extended Climate and Yield Observations (1997-2025)
proposals.motivation: Crop model projections of climate change impacts on wheat yields show high variability, undermining confidence in adaptation planning. Much of this uncertainty stems from models being calibrated on historical data ending in the mid-1990s, missing nearly three decades of observed climate extremes and agricultural responses. Updating these foundational datasets is critical for robust, actionable projections.
proposals.hypothesis: Calibrating a panel of wheat crop models (e.g., DSSAT, APSIM, CropSyst) with extended, post-1996 observational data will significantly reduce inter-model variability and improve the accuracy of near-term climate impact projections by better capturing crop responses to recent climatic trends and extremes.
proposals.method: We will compile a harmonized global dataset of observed wheat yields, management practices, and daily weather from 1997-2025. Following a standardized calibration protocol, we will systematically recalibrate key cultivar and soil parameters in multiple crop models for major wheat-growing environments. Model performance will be evaluated against the new data using statistical metrics (RMSE, d-index), and projected yields under recent (2006-2025) climate will be compared to pre-update projections to quantify changes in model agreement and bias.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a publicly available, updated calibration dataset and parameter set for major wheat models, directly addressing known sources of inter-model variability. It will provide a validated, near-term baseline for climate impact studies, improving the reliability of yield projections used by breeders and policymakers.
proposals.novelty: While multi-model studies are common, a systematic, protocol-driven recalibration of a model panel using the most recent 30 years of observed climate and yield data—a period containing distinct warming and extreme event trends—has not been comprehensively undertaken for global wheat systems.

proposals.title: Assessing the Stationarity of Crop Model Parameters for Wheat and Cassava Across Diverse Mega-Environments Using Panel Unit-Root Tests
proposals.motivation: Crop model calibration and intercomparison studies, such as the Agricultural Model Intercomparison and Improvement Project (AgMIP), have revealed significant variability in model performance and parameter estimates across different environments. Understanding whether this variability stems from non-stationary parameters—parameters that change systematically with environmental conditions—is critical for developing robust, widely applicable models. This is especially urgent for climate impact assessments and adaptation planning in vulnerable tropical and waterlogging-prone regions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that key phenological and stress-response parameters for major crops like wheat and cassava exhibit non-stationarity (i.e., possess unit roots) when analyzed across defined mega-environments, and that this statistical property explains a significant portion of the inter-model variability observed in previous studies.
proposals.method: We will apply the panel data and unit-root test framework from recent multi-model studies to a new set of crops (wheat, cassava) and geographical contexts. Using the concept of 'mega-environments'—clusters of locations with similar biotic and abiotic stresses—we will structure our panel data. For each crop, we will compile a multi-model, multi-location, multi-year panel dataset of calibrated parameters and yields. We will then implement the Im-Pesaran-Shin and Fisher-type panel unit-root tests to statistically evaluate the stationarity of critical parameters (e.g., photoperiod sensitivity, waterlogging stress factor) across these environmental clusters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal statistical diagnosis of parameter stability for key crops in understudied environments, directly addressing the question of 'why there is so much variability' in model outputs. It will yield a protocol for categorizing environments where universal parameters are insufficient, guiding future targeted calibration efforts and improving the portability of models for circular agriculture and climate resilience studies.
proposals.novelty: While panel unit-root tests have been discussed for crop model intercomparison, their systematic application to define parameter stability across biologically meaningful 'mega-environments' for multiple, contrasting crops (temperate cereal vs. tropical root crop) is novel. This moves beyond identifying variability to formally testing its structural cause within a rigorous climatic and agronomic framework.

proposals.title: A Machine Learning Framework to Decipher Non-Linear Climate-Yield Relationships in Wheat and Reduce Multi-Model Uncertainty
proposals.motivation: Process-based crop models exhibit significant variability in yield projections under climate stress, partly due to oversimplified representations of complex, non-linear climate-yield interactions, particularly for stresses like waterlogging. This uncertainty hinders reliable adaptation planning for wheat production systems. A data-driven approach is needed to explicitly quantify these complex relationships and inform model improvement.
proposals.hypothesis: Machine learning algorithms, specifically random forests and feed-forward neural networks, can identify and quantify non-linear, interactive effects of climate variables (e.g., temperature, precipitation extremes, vapor pressure deficit) on wheat yield more effectively than traditional regression or process-model ensembles, thereby explaining a key source of inter-model variability.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing multi-model simulation data from past studies (e.g., AgMIP Wheat) and observed yield data from diverse environments as training datasets. We will implement and compare a Random Forest regressor for feature importance analysis and interpretability, and a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) neural network to capture deeper non-linearities. Key climate variables and their interactions will be extracted from model input/output files and observational datasets, following a standardized protocol for defining stress events. The trained ML models will be analyzed to identify critical climate thresholds and interaction effects missed by conventional models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, empirically-grounded mapping of critical climate-yield relationships for wheat, directly addressing sources of model variability identified in prior research. The resulting insights will contribute to a more targeted calibration protocol for process-based models, focusing on the parameters governing responses to the identified non-linear stresses, ultimately improving yield projection reliability.
proposals.novelty: While ML has been applied for yield prediction, its targeted use to diagnose specific non-linear climate interactions *within* the context of multi-model analysis and to directly inform the parameterization and reduction of uncertainty in process-based crop models is a novel synthesis. This bridges data-driven and mechanistic modeling paradigms.

proposals.title: Spatial Econometric Calibration of a Wheat Model to Account for Yield Interdependence and Climate Correlations Across a Regional Network
proposals.motivation: Multi-model studies, including those by Hoogenboom and colleagues, highlight significant unexplained variability in crop yield predictions, partly due to ignoring spatial dependencies. In regions like the US Midwest or the North China Plain, yields in neighboring prefectures are not independent; they are linked by shared climate extremes, pest movements, and management practices, violating standard statistical assumptions in model calibration and evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling spatial yield interdependence using spatial econometric methods will significantly improve the calibration of wheat model parameters and reduce prediction error variance compared to traditional non-spatial calibration protocols.
proposals.method: We will implement a Spatial Autoregressive (SAR) or Spatial Durbin Model (SDM) framework within a crop model calibration workflow. Using historical wheat yield data and associated climate/soil inputs from a network of prefectures, we will estimate a spatial weight matrix based on contiguity and climate correlation. Model parameters (e.g., for phenology, stress response) will be calibrated by maximizing a likelihood function incorporating the spatial lag of yield, using pre-2026 algorithms such as Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) as implemented in the `spdep` R package. The protocol will be tested against traditional non-spatial calibration under diverse environments, including waterlogging-prone areas.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel calibration protocol that accounts for yield spillovers, leading to more robust and spatially coherent parameter sets for crop models. It will directly address a source of variability in multi-model studies and improve the accuracy of regional yield assessments under climate variability, particularly for interconnected agricultural landscapes.
proposals.novelty: While spatial statistics are used in agro-climatology, their integration into the formal parameter calibration process of process-based crop simulation models is novel. This moves beyond analyzing spatial patterns of model error to fundamentally restructuring the objective function based on spatial econometric theory.

proposals.title: Causal evaluation of waterlogging adaptation policies in wheat systems using synthetic control and crop model ensembles
proposals.motivation: Climate adaptation policies, such as subsidies for drainage or waterlogging-tolerant varieties, are being implemented in key wheat-growing regions, but their causal impact on yield stability remains poorly quantified. Pre-2026 crop model intercomparisons reveal high variability in simulated responses to environmental stresses like waterlogging, complicating traditional ex-ante policy analysis. A rigorous, data-driven evaluation is needed to inform effective agricultural policy under climate uncertainty.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the implementation of targeted waterlogging adaptation policies in specific agro-climatic zones has caused a statistically significant increase in observed wheat yield resilience, an effect that can be isolated from confounding climate trends using causal inference methods.
proposals.method: We will apply the Synthetic Control Method (SCM) to evaluate real-world policies. Treated units (regions with policy implementation) will be matched with a weighted combination of control units (similar regions without the policy) using pre-intervention covariates, including historical climate data, soil properties, and management practices. The causal effect will be estimated as the difference in post-intervention yield trends between treated and synthetic control regions. We will leverage the AgMIP multi-model ensemble protocol to generate robust counterfactual yield distributions for validation, using pre-calibrated wheat models (e.g., DSSAT, APSIM) to simulate expected yields under observed weather, both with and without the policy's management adaptations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, causal evidence base for climate adaptation policy effectiveness in agriculture, moving beyond correlation. It will demonstrate a methodological framework that integrates econometric causal inference with process-based crop modeling, directly addressing the challenge of variability in model studies highlighted in prior AgMIP research.
proposals.novelty: While causal methods are established in economics, their application to evaluate real-world agri-environmental policies using a fusion of observational data and mechanistic crop model ensembles is novel. This approach moves past model sensitivity analysis to a direct, data-centric policy evaluation grounded in causal identification.

proposals.title: Calibrating a Wheat Crop Model to Farm-Level Micro-Panel Data to Quantify Heterogeneous Climate Impacts Within a Prefecture
proposals.motivation: Regional-scale crop model assessments often mask critical sub-regional heterogeneity in climate impacts, leading to suboptimal adaptation strategies for farmers. Pre-2026, the use of aggregated data or a few representative sites fails to capture farm-level variability in soil, management, and microclimate, which is a key source of uncertainty identified in multi-model studies. This gap limits the precision of climate adaptation protocols for major crops like wheat.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that calibrating a process-based wheat model (e.g., DSSAT) using a panel of high-resolution, multi-year farm-level yield and management data will reveal significant, systematic within-prefecture heterogeneity in crop response to climate stressors, particularly temperature extremes and waterlogging.
proposals.method: We will apply a panel data econometric approach to a micro-panel dataset of individual wheat farm yields, management records, and local weather from a prefecture over 5-10 years. The CSM-CERES-Wheat model within the DSSAT framework will be calibrated not for a single 'average' farm, but for clusters of farms identified via unsupervised learning (e.g., k-means clustering on soil and topographic variables). A multi-site calibration protocol will be used to estimate distinct parameter sets for each cluster, focusing on sensitive parameters related to phenology, root growth, and waterlogging stress, informed by prior sensitivity analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, fine-scale map of climate impact vulnerability for wheat production at the sub-prefecture level. It will directly contribute to reducing unexplained variability in crop model inter-comparisons by demonstrating how accounting for farm-level heterogeneity improves predictive skill. The resulting cluster-specific parameter sets will enable more targeted extension advice and breeding program design for specific mega-environments.
proposals.novelty: While panel methods have been used with regional data, applying them to calibrate a biophysical crop model at the individual farm level is novel. This bridges the gap between econometric impact studies and process-based crop modeling, moving beyond representative farms to a continuous, data-driven characterization of landscape heterogeneity.

proposals.title: A Global Multi-Model, Multi-Country Analysis of Wheat Yield Sensitivity to Climate Extremes: Calibrating for Waterlogging and Heat Stress
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 multi-model studies, including those by Hoogenboom, reveal high variability in climate impact projections, complicating adaptation planning. This variability is partly due to inconsistent model parameterization and a lack of standardized protocols for global comparative analysis. A systematic, large-scale calibration effort is needed to quantify and reduce uncertainty in national-level yield sensitivity, especially for compound stresses like heat and waterlogging prevalent in post-rice wheat systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implementing a unified calibration protocol for key phenological and stress-response parameters across a global multi-model ensemble will significantly reduce inter-model variability and reveal distinct, quantifiable mega-environments of climate sensitivity for wheat at the national scale.
proposals.method: We will employ the Agricultural Model Intercomparison and Improvement Project (AgMIP) protocols to create a harmonized global panel dataset of weather, soil, and management data for major wheat-producing nations. The DSSAT-CROPSIM-CERES wheat models will be calibrated using a multi-environment trial (MET) approach, focusing on parameters for vernalization, photoperiod, and waterlogging stress identified as critical in prior work. Sensitivity analysis will be conducted using the Global Sensitivity Analysis (GSA) method (Sobol indices) to rank parameter influence on yield variance under historical climate extremes (1980-2020).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first globally consistent, multi-country dataset of calibrated wheat model parameters, directly addressing sources of variability in impact assessments. It will deliver a map of wheat mega-environments defined by climate stress sensitivity, providing a robust evidence base for targeting breeding programs and climate-resilient agricultural policies internationally.
proposals.novelty: While multi-model studies exist, none have applied a standardized calibration protocol for stress parameters like waterlogging at a global scale to facilitate direct cross-country comparison of climate sensitivity. This moves beyond regional case studies to a systematic global diagnostic framework.

proposals.title: Dynamic Baseline Periods for Crop Model Calibration: Quantifying the Impact of Recent Climate Variability on Wheat Yield Projections
proposals.motivation: Crop model calibration and yield gap analysis often use a single, static baseline year (e.g., 2000) to represent historical climate, an assumption increasingly challenged by observed climate variability and trends. This practice, as highlighted in multi-model studies, may introduce significant bias in parameter estimation and subsequent yield projections under future climates, particularly for stress-sensitive processes like waterlogging. A robust, climate-aware calibration protocol is needed to improve model reliability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that calibrating wheat crop models using a dynamic, rolling baseline period that accounts for recent climate variability will yield a more robust and transferable parameter set, reducing projection uncertainty across diverse future environments compared to parameters derived from a single static year.
proposals.method: We will leverage the existing protocol from Hoogenboom's team for multi-environment wheat trials, integrating historical weather data (1980-2025). Instead of a single year, we will define sequential 10-year rolling baseline periods (e.g., 1991-2000, 1992-2001, up to 2016-2025). The DSSAT-CERES-Wheat model will be calibrated for each period using the Generalized Likelihood Uncertainty Estimation (GLUE) algorithm. Model performance and parameter stability will be evaluated across these periods and validated against independent trial data, with a focus on yield and waterlogging stress responses.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, empirically grounded calibration protocol that explicitly accounts for climate non-stationarity, directly addressing a key source of variability in crop multi-model studies. It will deliver a more climate-resilient parameter set for wheat models, enhancing the credibility of yield projections for climate adaptation planning in commercial agriculture.
proposals.novelty: While the use of multi-year data for calibration is common, the systematic evaluation of a moving baseline to explicitly isolate and incorporate the signal of recent climate trends into crop model parameters is a novel methodological advance, moving beyond the assumption of a stable representative climate.

proposals.title: A Remote Sensing-Assisted Protocol for Regional Calibration of Wheat Crop Models to Reduce Yield Prediction Variability
proposals.motivation: Multi-model ensembles for crop yield prediction show high variability, partly due to uncertain parameterization across diverse environments. This limits their utility for regional agricultural planning under climate change. Satellite-derived data on phenology and yield offer a consistent, spatially extensive source of information for model evaluation, but a systematic protocol for its integration into crop model calibration is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: That assimilating satellite-derived phenology (e.g., green-up, senescence) and end-of-season yield proxies into a multi-model calibration framework will significantly reduce inter-model variability and improve the regional accuracy of wheat yield simulations under contrasting water and nitrogen management regimes.
proposals.method: We will use the GLAM, DSSAT, and APSIM wheat models, applying a standardized calibration protocol across major U.S. wheat-producing regions. Satellite data (MODIS/ Landsat 8 for phenology, integrated with Sentinel-2 for higher resolution; SMAP for soil moisture) will be processed to extract key phenological dates and end-of-season yield proxies. A Bayesian calibration approach will be employed to update critical cultivar and soil parameters (e.g., phenological coefficients, root distribution, waterlogging stress parameters) by minimizing the discrepancy between simulated and remotely sensed state variables.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, transparent calibration protocol that leverages open-access remote sensing data to constrain crop models regionally. It will directly address sources of inter-model variability identified in prior studies and provide validated, spatially explicit yield simulations for assessing climate adaptation strategies in wheat-based systems.
proposals.novelty: While remote sensing has been used for model validation, its systematic integration into a multi-model Bayesian calibration protocol, specifically targeting the reduction of ensemble variability and incorporating stress factors like waterlogging, represents a significant methodological advance beyond current common practice.

ideas.title: Extrapolation Validation via Material Science Failure Models
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of methodological agreement for temperature increases beyond the historical range by drawing an analogy from materials science, where predictive models for material failure under extreme stress are not based on simple extrapolation from normal conditions. The analogous solution involves controlled failure testing to map non-linear stress-strain curves and identify failure mechanisms. Adapted to crop yield research, this means designing experiments that intentionally expose crops to extreme, failure-inducing temperatures in controlled environments to empirically map the non-linear yield response curve and identify the specific physiological breakdown points. This data would then be used to test when and why different methodological approaches (e.g., statistical models vs. process-based models) diverge in their predictions, moving beyond mere extrapolation of historical relationships.

ideas.title: Adapt a hydrological ensemble data assimilation method for soil moisture initialization in sowing models
ideas.description: This expanded idea proposes to solve the challenge of precisely initializing a soil water balance model for sowing date optimization by drawing an analogy from operational hydrology. In flood forecasting, ensemble data assimilation methods like the Ensemble Kalman Filter are routinely used to ingest observational data and correct model states for improved prediction. We adapt this solution by developing a framework to assimilate spatially distributed soil moisture observations (from satellites and ground networks) and rainfall data into the soil water balance model component. This process generates a probabilistic, physically consistent estimate of the initial soil water profile at the onset of the planting season. This superior initialization, when integrated with the ClimProb climate analysis and the CROPGRO crop model, will significantly reduce the uncertainty stemming from unknown initial conditions, leading to more robust and location-specific sowing recommendations for rainfed agriculture.

ideas.title: Applying Micro-Meteorological Downscaling and Ensemble Fusion to Farm-Level Vulnerability
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from micro-meteorology, where high-resolution local forecasts are created by downscaling global models. The analogous problem—predicting local weather extremes—is solved by using a multi-method ensemble that combines dynamical and statistical downscaling, blending large-scale climate forcing with fine-scale topographic and land-use data. Adapted to the original research context, the solution involves applying a similar multi-method ensemble at high resolution to identify local hotspots of climate vulnerability. This is achieved by nesting farm-scale impact models within regional climate model ensembles and fusing their outputs with hyper-local geospatial variables (e.g., soil composition, irrigation access, micro-topography). The result is a probabilistic, high-resolution mapping of vulnerability that quantifies uncertainty from both climate projections and local heterogeneities, effectively 'downscaling' impact assessments to the sub-national or individual farm level.

ideas.title: Robust optimization of sowing dates under non-linear, extreme-temperature yield shocks
ideas.description: This research integrates robust optimization with models that feature non-linear yield responses to extreme temperatures. The core innovation is to define the 'uncertainty set' for the robust optimization not just on climate variables, but also on the parameters of the yield response function itself, particularly in the extreme tail. This allows the optimization to identify sowing schedules that are robust both to worst-case weather realizations and to worst-case assumptions about how yields catastrophically decline at unprecedented heat levels, directly addressing where current methodological agreement breaks down.

ideas.title: Apply Portfolio Optimization from Finance to Crop Sowing Schedules
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from robust portfolio optimization in finance to the agricultural problem of selecting sowing dates under climate uncertainty. In finance, portfolios are constructed to perform resiliently across adverse market scenarios by diversifying across assets with uncorrelated risks. The analogous solution is applied by conceptualizing different sowing dates or crop varieties as 'assets' in a 'sowing portfolio.' A robust optimization model is then developed to allocate planting effort across this portfolio. The objective is not to maximize average expected yield, but to find the sowing strategy that secures the best possible guaranteed outcome (a max-min or min-max regret criterion) against a pre-defined uncertainty set representing worst-case climate scenarios, such as sequences of droughts, floods, or unseasonal temperatures.

ideas.title: Constraining CO2 fertilization uncertainty with observational and experimental priors for robust yield projections
ideas.description: This research first decomposes the uncertainty in yield projections arising specifically from the representation of the CO2 fertilization effect (eCFE) across models and empirical methods. It then formally constrains these divergent eCFE estimates using a Bayesian framework that incorporates observational data from historical yield trends and direct physiological responses from FACE experiments as informative priors. The output is a posterior distribution of plausible eCFE parameters for each modeling approach, which is then used to produce a multi-method ensemble of future yield projections. This results in a probabilistically weighted projection that is robust to the structural uncertainties in representing this key process.

ideas.title: Incorporate adaptation as a feedback parameter in ensemble projections using agent-based modeling
ideas.description: Future work should integrate deliberate human adaptation strategies into the multi-method ensemble to improve impact estimates. Drawing an analogy from epidemiology, where agent-based models dynamically simulate behavioral feedback (like vaccination uptake) in response to disease spread projections, this idea adapts that solution. The adapted research idea is to incorporate adaptation as a feedback parameter in ensemble projections using agent-based modeling. This involves treating adaptation strategies not as static inputs but as dynamic responses within the modeling framework. A decision-modeling layer would be integrated where the probability and magnitude of adaptation actions (e.g., infrastructure changes, agricultural shifts) are triggered by specific impact thresholds from the ensemble's projections. These adaptive actions then feed back to modify the subsequent climate impact trajectories within the ensemble, creating a coupled system that more realistically represents the interplay between physical climate risks and societal responses.

ideas.title: Diagnosing the drivers of multi-method disagreement in local climate impact hotspots
ideas.description: This research fuses the analysis of methodological divergence with high-resolution impact assessment to investigate why multi-method ensembles produce conflicting projections for specific sub-national or farm-level hotspots of vulnerability. By applying the ensemble at a granular scale to regions like Russia's agricultural belts, it will pinpoint where and how methods disagree—whether due to input data, parameterization, or model structure—in the very locations where decision-making is most sensitive. This enables a causal, place-based diagnosis of uncertainty sources, moving beyond broad regional comparisons to generate actionable insights for improving methodological coherence where it matters most for adaptation.

ideas.title: Adopt Regression Discontinuity Design from Political Science for Precision Agriculture Causal Inference
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Regression Discontinuity Design (RDD), a powerful causal inference method from political science and program evaluation, to the context of statistical yield modeling in agriculture. In political science, RDD is used to estimate causal effects when treatment assignment is determined by whether a unit's score on a continuous variable (e.g., a poverty index, vote share) falls above or below a specific cutoff. The analogous agricultural problem is evaluating the causal impact of an agronomic intervention (like a precision input application) that is triggered by a measurable threshold (e.g., a normalized difference vegetation index (NDVI) value, a soil conductivity reading). The adapted solution involves designing observational studies or analyzing existing precision agriculture data where such thresholds dictate management actions. By modeling yield as a function of the running variable and a treatment indicator, and comparing outcomes for units arbitrarily close to the cutoff, researchers can obtain a locally unbiased estimate of the causal effect of the intervention, significantly strengthening causal claims beyond standard correlational models.

ideas.title: Ensemble Framework for Co-Projecting Climate Impacts and Adaptive Response Uncertainties
ideas.description: This research develops a multi-method ensemble framework that co-projects climate impacts and human adaptation as interdependent, deeply uncertain processes. It integrates diverse models of adaptation pathways—spanning different assumptions about policy effectiveness, behavioral change, equity, and investment—directly into the ensemble structure. This allows for the systematic propagation of both adaptive benefits and maladaptive risks (e.g., increased inequality, lock-in, or transboundary effects) into final impact estimates. The output is a probabilistic characterization of future impacts that explicitly quantifies the influence and uncertainty of human societal responses, moving beyond the assumption of adaptation as a simple, deterministic offset.

ideas.title: Extending Ensemble Uncertainty Analysis via Pharmacokinetic Dose-Extrapolation Methods
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to extend ensemble uncertainty analysis for climate impacts to scenarios beyond 1°C of global warming, where direct model data is sparser. Drawing an analogy from pharmacology, where dose-response models are extrapolated to high doses with quantified uncertainty, the solution is to adapt pharmacokinetic dose-extrapolation methods. Specifically, we will fit empirical response functions to the existing multi-model ensemble data for the 0-1°C period. By extrapolating these functions to higher warming levels (e.g., 2°C, 3°C), we can formally quantify the total method uncertainty. This uncertainty will integrate the propagated parametric uncertainty from the fitted functions, the ensemble spread represented in the fitting data, and a crucial additional term representing the structural error or breakdown of the assumed functional form when extended beyond its calibrated range, providing a more robust and transparent uncertainty estimate for high-warming scenarios.

ideas.title: Causal Graph Discovery with Targeted Experimental Validation for Robust Agricultural Inference
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond applying off-the-shelf econometric methods by first using causal discovery algorithms on rich observational data (e.g., satellite imagery, soil sensors, management records) to map the plausible causal relationships and highlight the key, often untestable, assumptions required for IV or DiD. It then embeds a mechanism for active validation, designing and implementing small-scale, randomized experiments specifically to stress-test those critical assumptions. The results from these targeted experiments are used to either confirm the causal model, adjust it, or quantify the degree of bias from potential violations, leading to a far more robust and empirically grounded causal estimate than methods relying on untestable assumptions alone.

ideas.title: Modeling Emergent Sub-Optimality in Farming Systems via Constrained Agent-Based Decision Making
ideas.description: This research develops an agent-based simulation framework where farmer agents operate under heterogeneous and realistic constraints (e.g., capital, labor, information access, risk aversion). Instead of applying random stochasticity to management actions, sub-optimal practices like delayed weeding emerge endogenously from the agents' constrained optimization within their specific context. The model incorporates causal pathways from socio-economic factors to field-level outcomes, enabling the identification of which constraints most significantly drive yield gaps. This allows for the systematic testing of targeted interventions (e.g., information campaigns, micro-finance) to improve resilience and productivity in simulated farming systems.

ideas.title: Causal Discovery-Augmented SEM for Robustly Quantifying Temperature Effect Pathways on Yield
ideas.description: This approach combines causal discovery algorithms with structural equation modeling to overcome the critical flaw of relying on a single, potentially misspecified causal graph. It first uses data-driven methods to generate and evaluate a set of statistically credible causal structures linking temperature, yield, and key intermediate variables. Then, it employs this ensemble of models in a formal robustness analysis, explicitly reporting how the estimated direct and indirect effects of temperature vary across the space of plausible causal assumptions. This produces a more reliable and transparent quantification of temperature's pathways of influence, with explicit uncertainty bounds stemming from causal structure uncertainty.

ideas.title: Bayesian Hierarchical Framework for Robust Estimation and Uncertainty Quantification of Cultivar-Specific Genetic Coefficients
ideas.description: This research develops a Bayesian hierarchical modeling system to overcome the ill-posed inverse problem in estimating genetic coefficients for unlisted cultivars. The framework incorporates strong, physiologically informed prior distributions and leverages phylogenetic relatedness between cultivars to share statistical strength, constraining plausible parameter space. It uses MCMC sampling to produce full posterior distributions for each coefficient, explicitly quantifying estimation uncertainty and identifiability. This approach ensures that resulting parameter sets are both biologically credible and accompanied by clear metrics of confidence for use in model applications like climate impact studies or breeding design.

ideas.title: Adapt Ensemble Data Assimilation from Numerical Weather Prediction for Multi-Method Projection Averaging
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to a core problem in Numerical Weather Prediction (NWP)—combining multiple, imperfect model forecasts into a superior probabilistic prediction—to the challenge of combining projections from different methodological classes in climate science or other long-term forecasting domains. The analogous solution is ensemble data assimilation, which uses techniques like the Ensemble Kalman Filter to formally weight ensemble members based on their uncertainty and consistency with incoming data. The adapted research idea applies this dynamic, sequential Bayesian framework to multi-method projection averaging. It proposes to move beyond static Bayesian Model Averaging by developing a system that continuously updates the weights assigned to different method classes (e.g., process-based models, statistical models, expert elicitations) as new observational constraints become available and the projection horizon evolves, creating a more responsive and evidence-weighted consensus projection.

ideas.title: Emulate climate model ensembles with neural operators for rapid parameter sensitivity analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from climate science, where neural operators are trained on ensembles of expensive Earth System Model simulations to enable rapid uncertainty exploration and sensitivity analysis. The analogous solution involves using deep learning emulators to learn the mapping from model parameters to spatio-temporal outputs, bypassing the need for full simulations. Adapted to the context of grid-based process models (e.g., in geosciences or engineering), we propose training a neural operator architecture (such as a Fourier Neural Operator) on a diverse ensemble of simulation runs. This emulator will capture the complex, high-dimensional physics, allowing for instantaneous sampling over the joint parameter space. This enables previously infeasible tasks like comprehensive global sensitivity analysis, robust uncertainty quantification, and rapid probabilistic forecasting, all at a computational cost dwarfed by that of the original numerical model.

ideas.title: Incorporate Technological Regime Shifts via Ecological Succession Models
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of stable management and technology trends by adapting a solution from ecology. In ecology, succession models solve the analogous problem of predicting complex system evolution by mapping transitions between distinct community states (e.g., after a fire), rather than extrapolating from a stable point. The adapted research idea incorporates projections of future technological change by defining analogous 'technological regimes' and using modified succession models to simulate the non-linear, probabilistic transitions between them—including triggers like breakthrough innovations or policy changes—within statistical and simulation models of economic growth, organizational strategy, or infrastructure planning.

ideas.title: Scenario-Based Morphological Exploration for Technological Deep Uncertainty
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to replace deterministic technology projections with a structured exploration of deep uncertainty. It uses morphological analysis and scenario generation to define a bounded yet diverse set of coherent, non-probabilistic future technological states, informed by expert elicitation on discontinuities and adoption barriers. Statistical and simulation models are then executed across this full scenario ensemble to identify policy and investment strategies that are robust—performing adequately across multiple plausible futures—rather than those optimized for a single, likely flawed, forecast. The output is a map of decision resilience under technological uncertainty, not a precise prediction.

ideas.title: Causal Emulation for Robust Counterfactual Analysis in Complex Systems
ideas.description: This research direction integrates causal inference methods (e.g., instrumental variables, difference-in-differences) directly into the architecture and training objectives of machine learning emulators for complex process models. Instead of training an emulator to merely mimic a simulation's outputs, it is trained to learn the underlying structural equations or potential outcomes framework implied by a chosen causal design. This allows the fast, uncertainty-exploring emulator to generate not just predictions, but valid counterfactual estimates (e.g., the effect of a new agricultural intervention) across vast parameter spaces, while rigorously accounting for confounding. The result is a powerful tool for policy design that combines the scalability of ML with the credibility of econometric causal identification.

ideas.title: Structural Discrepancy-Weighted Multi-Ensemble Projection Framework
ideas.description: This framework moves beyond simple Bayesian model averaging by explicitly accounting for structural uncertainty across different methodological classes. It first applies process-based adequacy tests to filter out models that fail to reproduce key known mechanisms. The projections from the surviving models are then combined using BMA, but the final uncertainty distribution is inflated by a structural discrepancy term. This term is derived from the irreducible spread between the central estimates of distinct, validated model classes, ensuring the final projection intervals are robust to shared biases and missing model types.

ideas.title: A Pre-Registered Framework for Context-Driven Method Selection to Harmonize Regional Impact Assessments
ideas.description: This research develops and tests a structured, pre-registration protocol to force explicit justification of methodological choices for regional impact assessments before analysis is conducted. It creates a decision-tree tool that maps regional data characteristics, institutional contexts, and research questions to recommended methodological pathways, making assumptions and trade-offs transparent. The framework is validated by applying it prospectively to contentious cases like Russia and other high-divergence regions, then comparing the coherence of resulting assessments against traditional post-hoc analyses of divergence.

ideas.title: Adapt Delphi Method from Technology Forecasting for Methodological Ensemble Weighting
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Delphi method, a classic technique from technology forecasting and foresight, to the problem of quantifying method uncertainty. In the analogous field, the Delphi method is used to structure expert elicitation over multiple iterative rounds with controlled feedback to converge on quantified forecasts. The adapted solution applies this structured process to formally elicit expert judgments for weighting different methodological approaches in an ensemble. Experts would provide initial probabilistic weights, receive anonymized summaries of the panel's distributions and rationales, and then revise their judgments over several rounds. This process quantifies the uncertainty inherent in methodological choice, mitigates biases from dominant individuals, and produces a robust, consensus-informed weighting scheme to improve the ensemble's reliability and interpretability.
